source.subject: Customer kickoff: schedule, attendees, agenda & meeting links
messages:
From: Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir@medipointhealth.com>, Emily Zhao <emily@novaplace.io>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 09 Sep 2026 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: Customer kickoff: proposed times, attendee roster, and draft agenda

Hi Samir / Emily —

Thanks again for agreeing to a kickoff to get MediPoint onto Redwood. I put together a short draft agenda and a few time options below. Please let me know preferred time and which of your team members will join (technical, security, product owner). Also flag any additions to the agenda.

Proposed dates/times (PT):
- Wed, Sep 16 — 10:00–11:00am PT (preferred)
- Thu, Sep 17 — 1:00–2:00pm PT
- Mon, Sep 21 — 9:00–10:00am PT

Draft agenda (45–60m):
- 10m — Introductions & success criteria (Customer + Redwood)
- 15m — Product overview: deployment modes and expected timeline (Connor)
- 15m — Implementation plan: SSO, VPC/networking, data flow & access (Monica/Ben)
- 10m — Next steps: mutual action plan, owners, and dates

Prework / asks before the call:
- Share list of attendees + roles (technical owner, security/compliance, product owner)
- Any network contacts for SSO/VPN or person who will provision infra
- If you have an internal runbook or security questionnaire, attach or drop a link

Attached: kickoff-agenda-v1.pdf (short agenda + sample MAP). I can iterate once you confirm attendees.

Best,
Connor O'Brien
Technical Account Manager, Redwood Inference
connor@redwood.com
[Attachment: kickoff-agenda-v1.pdf (PDF, 120 KB)]

From: Samir Patel <samir@medipointhealth.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
Cc: Emily Zhao <emily@novaplace.io>
Date: Thu, 09 Sep 2026 18:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Customer kickoff: proposed times, attendee roster, and draft agenda

Connor —

Thanks for the clear plan. Sep 16 at 10:00am PT works best for our core team. I'll bring:
- Samir Patel — Head of Data Platform (me)
- Emily Zhao — Product Manager (cc'd)
- David Kim (I'll loop him in for the call) — our SRE lead for integrations

David's email is david@medipointhealth.com — please include him on the calendar invite.

A couple quick asks from my side:
- Can we confirm the expected sign-off owners for SSO vs infrastructure?
- We have an internal SOC2 questionnaire; should I send that ahead of time or bring it up on the call?

I'll attach the SOC2 questionnaire to my next email if you'd prefer it in advance. Otherwise Sep 16 at 10am is confirmed on our end.

Thanks,
Samir
Head of Data Platform, MediPoint Health
samir@medipointhealth.com

From: Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 09 Sep 2026 20:10:00 -0700
Subject: INTERNAL: MediPoint kickoff — staffing and run-of-show

Team —

Confirmed: MediPoint kickoff on Sep 16, 10:00am PT. External attendees: Samir (Head of Data), Emily (PM), David (SRE).

Proposed internal roster and asks:
- Connor (me) — product overview + MAP ownership
- Monica — implementation plan deep-dive (SSO, VPC expectations, audit/logging requirements)
- Ben — run through onboarding steps: environment access, tokens/keys, test dataset, and demo flow
- Jordan — take notes, capture action items in the MAP (I can assign follow-ups in HubSpot)

Pre-call prep:
- Monica: bring SSO checklist and expected time-to-complete for our side (5–10m)
- Ben: prepare the short demo (5–7m) and list of required customer privileges
- Jordan: create HubSpot task template we can use on the call (link to be shared)

Attaching the customer playbook draft I started — I'll fill in the MAP post-call.

Connor
[Attachment: MediPoint-onboarding-playbook.xlsx (XLSX, 420 KB)]

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir@medipointhealth.com>, David Kim <david@medipointhealth.com>, Emily Zhao <emily@novaplace.io>, Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Sep 2026 08:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Customer kickoff: proposed times, attendee roster, and draft agenda — calendar invite + bridge

Hi all —

Thanks for confirming. I've created the calendar invite for Wed, Sep 16 at 10:00–11:00am PT and added a Zoom link + Redwood conference bridge. Please see details below and let me know if you need an alternate dial-in.

Meeting: MediPoint x Redwood — Kickoff
When: Sep 16, 2026 · 10:00–11:00am PT
Where: Zoom (see link) + Redwood Bridge
Zoom: https://zoom.us/j/9876543210?pwd=redwoodkickoff
Bridge: +1-650-555-0123 PIN 4829
Calendar: attached (redwood-calendar-invite.ics)

Agenda is the same as Connor shared — we'll allocate time per the draft. Samir — if you want to circulate the SOC2 questionnaire ahead of the call, we can have Monica review and surface any items to the security team after the call.

On Redwood's side:
- Connor: product + MAP
- Monica: SSO/networking/security approach
- Ben: demo & access checklist
- Jordan: MAP + action item tracking

Looking forward to it.

Best,
Ben Carter
Customer Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood-calendar-invite.ics (ICS)]

On Thu, Sep 9, 2026 at 8:10 PM Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com> wrote:
> Team —
> 
> Confirmed: MediPoint kickoff on Sep 16, 10:00am PT. External attendees: Samir (Head of Data), Emily (PM), David (SRE).
> 
> Proposed internal roster and asks:...

To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
Cc: Emily Zhao <emily@novaplace.io>, David Kim <david@medipointhealth.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Customer kickoff: proposed times, attendee roster, and draft agenda — calendar invite + bridge

Perfect — thank you, Ben and Connor. I got the invite and will send the SOC2 questionnaire by COB today. David will join the call. See you on Sep 16.

Samir
Head of Data Platform, MediPoint Health

From: Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir@medipointhealth.com>, Emily Zhao <emily@novaplace.io>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 09 Sep 2026 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: Customer kickoff: proposed times, attendee roster, and draft agenda

Hi Samir / Emily —

Thanks again for agreeing to a kickoff to get MediPoint onto Redwood. I put together a short draft agenda and a few time options below. Please let me know preferred time and which of your team members will join (technical, security, product owner). Also flag any additions to the agenda.

Proposed dates/times (PT):
- Wed, Sep 16 — 10:00–11:00am PT (preferred)
- Thu, Sep 17 — 1:00–2:00pm PT
- Mon, Sep 21 — 9:00–10:00am PT

Draft agenda (45–60m):
- 10m — Introductions & success criteria (Customer + Redwood)
- 15m — Product overview: deployment modes and expected timeline (Connor)
- 15m — Implementation plan: SSO, VPC/networking, data flow & access (Monica/Ben)
- 10m — Next steps: mutual action plan, owners, and dates

Prework / asks before the call:
- Share list of attendees + roles (technical owner, security/compliance, product owner)
- Any network contacts for SSO/VPN or person who will provision infra
- If you have an internal runbook or security questionnaire, attach or drop a link

Attached: kickoff-agenda-v1.pdf (short agenda + sample MAP). I can iterate once you confirm attendees.

Best,
Connor O'Brien
Technical Account Manager, Redwood Inference
connor@redwood.com
[Attachment: kickoff-agenda-v1.pdf (PDF, 120 KB)]

From: Samir Patel <samir@medipointhealth.com>
To: Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
Cc: Emily Zhao <emily@novaplace.io>
Date: Thu, 09 Sep 2026 18:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Customer kickoff: proposed times, attendee roster, and draft agenda

Connor —

Thanks for the clear plan. Sep 16 at 10:00am PT works best for our core team. I'll bring:
- Samir Patel — Head of Data Platform (me)
- Emily Zhao — Product Manager (cc'd)
- David Kim (I'll loop him in for the call) — our SRE lead for integrations

David's email is david@medipointhealth.com — please include him on the calendar invite.

A couple quick asks from my side:
- Can we confirm the expected sign-off owners for SSO vs infrastructure?
- We have an internal SOC2 questionnaire; should I send that ahead of time or bring it up on the call?

I'll attach the SOC2 questionnaire to my next email if you'd prefer it in advance. Otherwise Sep 16 at 10am is confirmed on our end.

Thanks,
Samir
Head of Data Platform, MediPoint Health
samir@medipointhealth.com

From: Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 09 Sep 2026 20:10:00 -0700
Subject: INTERNAL: MediPoint kickoff — staffing and run-of-show

Team —

Confirmed: MediPoint kickoff on Sep 16, 10:00am PT. External attendees: Samir (Head of Data), Emily (PM), David (SRE).

Proposed internal roster and asks:
- Connor (me) — product overview + MAP ownership
- Monica — implementation plan deep-dive (SSO, VPC expectations, audit/logging requirements)
- Ben — run through onboarding steps: environment access, tokens/keys, test dataset, and demo flow
- Jordan — take notes, capture action items in the MAP (I can assign follow-ups in HubSpot)

Pre-call prep:
- Monica: bring SSO checklist and expected time-to-complete for our side (5–10m)
- Ben: prepare the short demo (5–7m) and list of required customer privileges
- Jordan: create HubSpot task template we can use on the call (link to be shared)

Attaching the customer playbook draft I started — I'll fill in the MAP post-call.

Connor
[Attachment: MediPoint-onboarding-playbook.xlsx (XLSX, 420 KB)]

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir@medipointhealth.com>, David Kim <david@medipointhealth.com>, Emily Zhao <emily@novaplace.io>, Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Sep 2026 08:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Customer kickoff: proposed times, attendee roster, and draft agenda — calendar invite + bridge

Hi all —

Thanks for confirming. I've created the calendar invite for Wed, Sep 16 at 10:00–11:00am PT and added a Zoom link + Redwood conference bridge. Please see details below and let me know if you need an alternate dial-in.

Meeting: MediPoint x Redwood — Kickoff
When: Sep 16, 2026 · 10:00–11:00am PT
Where: Zoom (see link) + Redwood Bridge
Zoom: https://zoom.us/j/9876543210?pwd=redwoodkickoff
Bridge: +1-650-555-0123 PIN 4829
Calendar: attached (redwood-calendar-invite.ics)

Agenda is the same as Connor shared — we'll allocate time per the draft. Samir — if you want to circulate the SOC2 questionnaire ahead of the call, we can have Monica review and surface any items to the security team after the call.

On Redwood's side:
- Connor: product + MAP
- Monica: SSO/networking/security approach
- Ben: demo & access checklist
- Jordan: MAP + action item tracking

Looking forward to it.

Best,
Ben Carter
Customer Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood-calendar-invite.ics (ICS)]

source.subject: GCP marketplace private offer — reseller crediting and burn schedule
messages:
From: CloudResell Ops <cpo@cloudresell.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Iris Wang <iris@acme.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 11:14:00 -04:00
Subject: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Hi Alex,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. Attached is a draft of the private offer terms for Acme AI (private_offer_terms.pdf). Acme confirmed the committed spend ($500,000) and asked us to propose a drawdown cadence that aligns to their procurement and forecast windows.

High level items we think matter for GCP co-sell / crediting integration: 
- Redemption window on the private offer (how long after activation they can redeem).
- Mapping of Redwood SKUs to GCP listing SKUs for usage attribution.
- Invoice phasing and how co-sell credits will be calculated (frontload vs amortize).

Can you share Redwood's preferred burn schedule options and who on your finance/partner team needs to be looped for the crediting mechanism? Our ops lead Jordan is available Wed/Thu for a quick sync.

Cheers,
Samir Patel
CloudResell Ops
CloudResell, Partnerships

--
Attachment: private_offer_terms.pdf (PDF, 248KB)
From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 11:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Marissa / Ben — forwarding CloudResell's note and the draft terms from Samir. Acme is committed to $500k. They want us to propose a drawdown cadence that syncs to their procurement (PO timing) and CloudResell's redemption window. Questions I want us to resolve before we respond: 

1) Preferred redemption window (90 vs 180 days). Finance prefers 90 for revenue recognition simplicity but partners sometimes ask for longer.
2) Do we support frontloaded crediting (larger co-sell credit in months 1-3) or amortized across the committed period?
3) SKU mapping: we need a small mapping sheet for CloudResell so GCP can attribute usage. Marissa, can you take the SKU mapping? Ben, please advise on crediting and invoice phasing.

I'll propose a short call with Jordan + Samir tomorrow.

Thanks — Alex
Alex Martinez
Director, GTM Partnerships
Redwood Inference
From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 13:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Looping in a draft SKU mapping (see sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx). Notes / proposal: 
- SKU mapping: map GCP listing SKUs to Redwood product families (text-gen, chat, embeddings, dedupe). Keep aggregation at product family level to reduce mapping churn.
- Redemption window: recommend 90 days for the private offer start date; call this the "redemption window" in the private-offer terms. 90 days keeps invoicing clean for us and aligns with typical reseller workflows.
- Crediting: propose a hybrid approach — 60% of partner credit frontloaded across months 1-2, 40% amortized over months 3-6. This gives CloudResell an early incentive while smoothing our recognition impact.
- Invoice phasing: Redwood receives usage reports via GCP monthly; we will reconcile partner crediting monthly and document offsets on our invoices to CloudResell for the committed drawdown.

Open items for Ben: what tax / accounting flags do we need to call out for co-sell credits treated as contra-revenue? Also confirm if we need a Master Credit Agreement or if contract language in the private_offers doc suffices.

Thanks,
Marissa
Sr. Manager, Marketplace Partnerships
From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 15:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Quick finance take: 
- Redemption window: 90 days is acceptable. We should document the exact trigger (offer activation date) and include a clause for exceptions with exec sign-off.
- Crediting treatment: co-sell credits will be recorded as contra-revenue. Frontloading 60/40 split is workable, but we need to ensure the partner-side payment terms (how CloudResell will be paid/credited) are explicit. If CloudResell expects a cash payout versus invoice offset, that changes our cash flow timing and requires treasury sign-off.
- SKU mapping: fine at product-family level. Please ensure the mapping includes the GCP SKU IDs (not just names) so attribution is granular enough for Google reports.
- Attachments: I'll need the final private_offer_terms.pdf and the PO from Acme to validate invoice triggers.

I'll be on the call at 10am PT tomorrow.

Ben Carter
Finance — Partnerships Billing
From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: CloudResell Ops <cpo@cloudresell.com>, Iris Wang <iris@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>, Procurement <procurement@acme.ai>
Date: Fri, 04 Sep 2026 16:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting — proposed schedule and next steps

Hi Samir / Iris — thanks for the draft. After internal review here is Redwood's proposed approach for Acme's $500k commit (deal RDW-2026-0047):

1) Redemption window: 90 days from private-offer activation.
2) Drawdown cadence (recommended):
   - Month 0 (activation): frontload 25% of expected committed units as a credit pool to CloudResell to enable early onboarding (up to $125k of usage value).
   - Months 1–2: additional 35% distributed across two months (~17.5% each).
   - Months 3–6: remaining 40% amortized evenly.
3) Crediting split: 60% of partner-eligible credit delivered in first two months (per Marissa's hybrid plan), 40% amortized months 3–6.
4) SKU mapping: Marissa attached sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx — please populate GCP SKU IDs where possible. We'll accept product-family mapping but GCP requires SKU IDs for attribution.
5) Invoicing / offsets: Redwood will reconcile monthly usage and apply partner credits as contra-revenue on invoices to CloudResell. If CloudResell requires cash payouts, we need a separate payout agreement and treasury approval.

Next steps:
- Samir: confirm that CloudResell can accept a 90-day redemption window and the frontload numbers above.
- Iris / Acme Procurement: please send the PO for the committed amount or confirm you will accept the private offer activation flow. If you can email PO to procurement@redwood.ai that will speed finance validation.
- Samir: if agreed, please route the final private offer through DocuSign (link recommended below) and attach signed MOP when complete.

We are happy to join a brief sync to finalize mapping and cadence. Proposed slot: Fri 9/4 10am PT (Jordan, Ben, Marissa, Alex available).

Best,
Alex Martinez
Director, GTM Partnerships
Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx (XLSX, 56KB)
Attachment: acme_po_0926.pdf (PDF, 102KB)
Attachment: signed_mop.docx (DOCX, 34KB)

Thanks for the quick intro last week. Attached is a draft of the private offer terms for Acme AI (private_offer_terms.pdf). Acme confirmed the committed spend ($500,000) and asked us to propose a drawdown cadence that aligns to their procurement and forecast windows.

High level items we think matter for GCP co-sell / crediting integration: 
- Redemption window on the private offer (how long after activation they can redeem).
- Mapping of Redwood SKUs to GCP listing SKUs for usage attribution.
- Invoice phasing and how co-sell credits will be calculated (frontload vs amortize).

Can you share Redwood's preferred burn schedule options and who on your finance/partner team needs to be looped for the crediting mechanism? Our ops lead Jordan is available Wed/Thu for a quick sync.

Cheers,
Samir Patel
CloudResell Ops
CloudResell, Partnerships

--
Attachment: private_offer_terms.pdf (PDF, 248KB)


From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 11:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Marissa / Ben — forwarding CloudResell's note and the draft terms from Samir. Acme is committed to $500k. They want us to propose a drawdown cadence that syncs to their procurement (PO timing) and CloudResell's redemption window. Questions I want us to resolve before we respond: 

1) Preferred redemption window (90 vs 180 days). Finance prefers 90 for revenue recognition simplicity but partners sometimes ask for longer.
2) Do we support frontloaded crediting (larger co-sell credit in months 1-3) or amortized across the committed period?
3) SKU mapping: we need a small mapping sheet for CloudResell so GCP can attribute usage. Marissa, can you take the SKU mapping? Ben, please advise on crediting and invoice phasing.

I'll propose a short call with Jordan + Samir tomorrow.

Thanks — Alex
Alex Martinez
Director, GTM Partnerships
Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 13:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Looping in a draft SKU mapping (see sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx). Notes / proposal: 
- SKU mapping: map GCP listing SKUs to Redwood product families (text-gen, chat, embeddings, dedupe). Keep aggregation at product family level to reduce mapping churn.
- Redemption window: recommend 90 days for the private offer start date; call this the "redemption window" in the private-offer terms. 90 days keeps invoicing clean for us and aligns with typical reseller workflows.
- Crediting: propose a hybrid approach — 60% of partner credit frontloaded across months 1-2, 40% amortized over months 3-6. This gives CloudResell an early incentive while smoothing our recognition impact.
- Invoice phasing: Redwood receives usage reports via GCP monthly; we will reconcile partner crediting monthly and document offsets on our invoices to CloudResell for the committed drawdown.

Open items for Ben: what tax / accounting flags do we need to call out for co-sell credits treated as contra-revenue? Also confirm if we need a Master Credit Agreement or if contract language in the private_offers doc suffices.

Thanks,
Marissa
Sr. Manager, Marketplace Partnerships


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 15:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting

Quick finance take: 
- Redemption window: 90 days is acceptable. We should document the exact trigger (offer activation date) and include a clause for exceptions with exec sign-off.
- Crediting treatment: co-sell credits will be recorded as contra-revenue. Frontloading 60/40 split is workable, but we need to ensure the partner-side payment terms (how CloudResell will be paid/credited) are explicit. If CloudResell expects a cash payout versus invoice offset, that changes our cash flow timing and requires treasury sign-off.
- SKU mapping: fine at product-family level. Please ensure the mapping includes the GCP SKU IDs (not just names) so attribution is granular enough for Google reports.
- Attachments: I'll need the final private_offer_terms.pdf and the PO from Acme to validate invoice triggers.

I'll be on the call at 10am PT tomorrow.

Ben Carter
Finance — Partnerships Billing


From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: CloudResell Ops <cpo@cloudresell.com>, Iris Wang <iris@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.ai>, Procurement <procurement@acme.ai>
Date: Fri, 04 Sep 2026 16:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Initial private-offer drawdown proposal and co-sell crediting — proposed schedule and next steps

Hi Samir / Iris — thanks for the draft. After internal review here is Redwood's proposed approach for Acme's $500k commit (deal RDW-2026-0047):

1) Redemption window: 90 days from private-offer activation.
2) Drawdown cadence (recommended):
   - Month 0 (activation): frontload 25% of expected committed units as a credit pool to CloudResell to enable early onboarding (up to $125k of usage value).
   - Months 1–2: additional 35% distributed across two months (~17.5% each).
   - Months 3–6: remaining 40% amortized evenly.
3) Crediting split: 60% of partner-eligible credit delivered in first two months (per Marissa's hybrid plan), 40% amortized months 3–6.
4) SKU mapping: Marissa attached sku_mapping_sheet.xlsx — please populate GCP SKU IDs where possible. We'll accept product-family mapping but GCP requires SKU IDs for attribution.
5) Invoicing / offsets: Redwood will reconcile monthly usage and apply partner credits as contra-revenue on invoices to CloudResell. If CloudResell requires cash payouts, we need a separate payout agreement and treasury approval.

Next steps:
- Samir: confirm that CloudResell can accept a 90-day redemption window and the frontload numbers above.
- Iris / Acme Procurement: please send the PO for the committed amount or confirm you will accept the private offer activation flow. If you can email PO to procurement@redwood.ai that will speed finance validation.
- Samir: if agreed, please route the final private offer through DocuSign (link recommended below) and attach signed MOP when complete.

We are happy to join a brief sync to finalize mapping and cadence. Proposed slot: Fri 9/4 10am PT (Jordan, Ben, Marissa, Alex available).

Best,
Alex Martinez
Director, GTM Partnerships
Redwood Inference

messages:
From: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Ben,

Thanks for the demo yesterday. Our security and network teams are evaluating how to connect our VPCs and on-prem datacenter to your Redwood Private control plane / inference nodes. Primary goals: private-only connectivity (no internet egress), predictable latency for inference (~<35ms ideally), and ability to support multiple AWS accounts and our on-prem CIDR space.

We have a relatively steady production load: roughly 5k-10k requests/sec during peak windows, average request payloads ~2KB, and occasional bulk batches that could spike sustained throughput to ~200 MB/s for short bursts.

Can you provide a recommendation: VPC Peering, AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints), or site-to-site VPN? We also want any operational caveats, HA/failover guidance, and rough cost assumptions.

If you prefer a quick call, we can book a 30m slot with our NetOps.

Thanks,
Tom
Tom Sullivan
Director, Cloud Networking
ACME Corp
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Tom —

Appreciate the details. Short answer: there isn't a single right option; each has tradeoffs. I can put together a short recommendation and cost/ops notes and send it back, or we can run a quick design call. If you can share whether you require transitive routing (i.e., do you want ACME VPCs to reach each other via Redwood's VPCs?) and whether Direct Connect is in scope, that will help.

I'll loop in our network engineers to get concrete configs and estimates.

-Ben
Ben Carter
Head of Solutions, Redwood Inference
From: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T10:14:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Ben — quick answers: no transitive routing desired (we don't want your infra to be a transit for our other VPCs), Direct Connect is not in scope for initial rollout (could be later). We'd prefer a written recommendation we can show to our security team. 30m call fine later if needed.

Thanks,
Tom
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Date: 2026-09-01T11:02:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Kim/Jordan — forwarding ACME ask. They want a concise recommendation (Peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN) with: 1) security boundary implications, 2) latency/bandwidth fit for their traffic profile (5k-10k req/s, occasional 200 MB/s bursts), 3) cross-account management (they have multiple AWS accounts), 4) failover plan for on-prem, and 5) an ops checklist for rollouts (endpoints per AZ, route table changes, monitoring).

Constraints: no transitive routing (per Tom), no Direct Connect initially, SOC2/compliance in play (so least-exposure to public internet is preferred). Attach two files: one-pager and their traffic estimates. Please reply-all with recommended approach and configuration details by EOD tomorrow — they'll likely include NetOps on the decision.

Forwarded message:
> From: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
> Date: 2026-09-01T09:12:00-07:00
> Subject: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request
> To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
>
> [original message quoted above]

Thanks,
Ben
From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Date: 2026-09-02T09:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Ben/Tom/Jordan —

High-level recommendation (summary):
- Primary: AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints) into a dedicated Redwood endpoint service backed by NLB(s), one endpoint per ACME account/region (security boundary preserved).
- Secondary failover: Site-to-site VPN to Redwood-managed VPN concentrators (IPSec BGP) for on-prem tolerance and as a fallback path for transient PrivateLink capacity limits.
- When sustained high bandwidth is required later, evaluate Direct Connect (or Partner Interconnect) as a long-term augmentation.

Why this combination?
- Security: PrivateLink provides the tightest security model — ACME creates an interface endpoint in their account that connects to our endpoint service; we do not need to add routes to ACME route tables that would create transitive routing. PrivateLink endpoints are customer-controlled and do not expose our VPC as a transit hub.
- Cross-account: PrivateLink is designed for multi-account consumption without sharing route tables. Creating an interface endpoint per account keeps IAM/policy scoping straightforward.
- Latency & throughput: Peering is lower-latency and supports high throughput without the SNAT/HA considerations of PrivateLink, but peering requires more route table management and is not suitable if you want to avoid transitive routing or if accounts are in different regions. PrivateLink adds ~1-3 ms extra latency in practice if NLBs are in the same AZ, and throughput is limited by ENI/NLB scaling. Given your 200 MB/s spike requirement, PrivateLink can handle it if we: (a) deploy NLBs in each AZ with cross-zone enabled, (b) configure automatic ENI scale-out and warm-up testing, and (c) recommend connection aggregation (multiple interface endpoints) for bandwidth headroom. For sustained >1 Gbps, propose Direct Connect.
- VPN fallback: Managed IPSec tunnels are cheap and provide predictable failover for on-prem paths; they do add latency and variable throughput based on the endpoints and the carrier. Use BGP to advertise networks and BFD for faster failure detection.

Operational checklist (concise):
- Create a Redwood endpoint service (NLB-backed) per region; register NLBs in multiple AZs.
- ACME creates one interface endpoint per account + region; confirm security group inbound rules limited to ACME subnets.
- DNS: Use Private DNS entries (aws VPC endpoint private DNS) or provide a dedicated internal hostname via Route53 resolver rules so that clients target the endpoint IPs directly.
- MTU: set MTU to 1500 and validate path MTU; some VPN/encapsulation paths may require 1400.
- Monitoring: CloudWatch for NLB TargetGroup healthy hosts, ENI metrics, Latency percentiles; VPC Flow Logs for troubleshooting; implement synthetic SLO probes for p99 latency and throughput.
- IAM & certs: Use endpoint policies to restrict which principals can connect; mutual TLS at application layer for request-level auth.
- Rollout: Pilot in single account with 1 AZ, measure throughput/p99 latency, then expand to multi-AZ and additional accounts.

Cost considerations (ballpark):
- PrivateLink: charges for NLB hours + Interface Endpoint hours + data processing ($/GB). For heavy egress patterns this is ~2-5x the raw data transfer of peering, but buys stronger isolation and simpler per-account controls.
- VPC Peering: low per-GB transfer (intra-region) and less per-hour overhead, but operational surface area grows (route tables, security groups, cross-account sharing). Not recommended if you explicitly want to avoid transitive routes.
- VPN: low fixed-hourly cost for AWS side tunnels + data transfer; can be constrained at high throughput and adds latency. Good as secondary/failover.

Configuration snippets to include in the writeup:
- NLB listeners (TCP 443), target group type = IP, health checks / 10s, deregistration delay 60s.
- Endpoint policy allowing only ACME account IDs to accept connections.
- BGP on VPN with advertisements limited to ACME on-prem CIDRs; keep routes specific.

Fallback & resilience:
- If PrivateLink NLB saturates in an AZ, traffic will rebalance to other AZs; ensure cross-zone enabled and autoscale NLB target capacity.
- Use DNS-based client retry/backoff and client-side connection pooling to avoid per-call connection churn.

I'll draft a 1-pager (attached) and a short rollout checklist. If you want, Jordan can sketch the NLB sizing math based on their traffic spreadsheet.

-Kimberly
Kimberly Park
Network Architect, Redwood Inference
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-03T16:40:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Tom —

Thanks for the patience. Per Kimberly: our recommendation is to lead with PrivateLink (interface endpoints) for initial rollout and use an IPSec site-to-site VPN as a failover path for on-prem connectivity. Key reasons: preserves your non-transitive constraint, gives account-level isolation, and aligns best with SOC2 expectations.

Next steps we suggest:
1) Pilot: create a PrivateLink endpoint in one ACME account + region, deploy Redwood NLBs across AZs, run load tests at your peak profile (5k-10k req/s, simulate 200 MB/s bursts).
2) Validate monitoring: p99 latency, NLB target metrics, ENI counts; iterate on NLB target scaling and client connection handling.
3) Configure IPSec VPN tunnels (BGP) to Redwood for on-prem fallback; test failover and MTU.
4) If sustained high bandwidth shows up, plan Direct Connect for the long term.

I've attached a one-page comparison and the spreadsheet with our initial capacity assumptions. Jordan will follow up with NLB sizing numbers if you want a more granular cost estimate.

Would you like us to open a shared Confluence / runbook and schedule a 30m tech call with NetOps this week?

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Head of Solutions, Redwood Inference
Attachments: privatelink-vs-peering-onepager.pdf, acme-mapping-traffic-estimates.xlsx

Subject: Fwd: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Kim/Jordan — forwarding ACME ask. They want a concise recommendation (Peering vs PrivateLink vs S2S VPN) with: 1) security boundary implications, 2) latency/bandwidth fit for their traffic profile (5k-10k req/s, occasional 200 MB/s bursts), 3) cross-account management (they have multiple AWS accounts), 4) failover plan for on-prem, and 5) an ops checklist for rollouts (endpoints per AZ, route table changes, monitoring).

Constraints: no transitive routing (per Tom), no Direct Connect initially, SOC2/compliance in play (so least-exposure to public internet is preferred). Attach two files: one-pager and their traffic estimates. Please reply-all with recommended approach and configuration details by EOD tomorrow — they'll likely include NetOps on the decision.

Forwarded message:
> From: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
> Date: 2026-09-01T09:12:00-07:00
> Subject: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request
> To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
>
> [original message quoted above]

Thanks,
Ben


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Date: 2026-09-02T09:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Ben/Tom/Jordan —

High-level recommendation (summary):
- Primary: AWS PrivateLink (interface endpoints) into a dedicated Redwood endpoint service backed by NLB(s), one endpoint per ACME account/region (security boundary preserved).
- Secondary failover: Site-to-site VPN to Redwood-managed VPN concentrators (IPSec BGP) for on-prem tolerance and as a fallback path for transient PrivateLink capacity limits.
- When sustained high bandwidth is required later, evaluate Direct Connect (or Partner Interconnect) as a long-term augmentation.

Why this combination?
- Security: PrivateLink provides the tightest security model — ACME creates an interface endpoint in their account that connects to our endpoint service; we do not need to add routes to ACME route tables that would create transitive routing. PrivateLink endpoints are customer-controlled and do not expose our VPC as a transit hub.
- Cross-account: PrivateLink is designed for multi-account consumption without sharing route tables. Creating an interface endpoint per account keeps IAM/policy scoping straightforward.
- Latency & throughput: Peering is lower-latency and supports high throughput without the SNAT/HA considerations of PrivateLink, but peering requires more route table management and is not suitable if you want to avoid transitive routing or if accounts are in different regions. PrivateLink adds ~1-3 ms extra latency in practice if NLBs are in the same AZ, and throughput is limited by ENI/NLB scaling. Given your 200 MB/s spike requirement, PrivateLink can handle it if we: (a) deploy NLBs in each AZ with cross-zone enabled, (b) configure automatic ENI scale-out and warm-up testing, and (c) recommend connection aggregation (multiple interface endpoints) for bandwidth headroom. For sustained >1 Gbps, propose Direct Connect.
- VPN fallback: Managed IPSec tunnels are cheap and provide predictable failover for on-prem paths; they do add latency and variable throughput based on the endpoints and the carrier. Use BGP to advertise networks and BFD for faster failure detection.

Operational checklist (concise):
- Create a Redwood endpoint service (NLB-backed) per region; register NLBs in multiple AZs.
- ACME creates one interface endpoint per account + region; confirm security group inbound rules limited to ACME subnets.
- DNS: Use Private DNS entries (aws VPC endpoint private DNS) or provide a dedicated internal hostname via Route53 resolver rules so that clients target the endpoint IPs directly.
- MTU: set MTU to 1500 and validate path MTU; some VPN/encapsulation paths may require 1400.
- Monitoring: CloudWatch for NLB TargetGroup healthy hosts, ENI metrics, Latency percentiles; VPC Flow Logs for troubleshooting; implement synthetic SLO probes for p99 latency and throughput.
- IAM & certs: Use endpoint policies to restrict which principals can connect; mutual TLS at application layer for request-level auth.
- Rollout: Pilot in single account with 1 AZ, measure throughput/p99 latency, then expand to multi-AZ and additional accounts.

Cost considerations (ballpark):
- PrivateLink: charges for NLB hours + Interface Endpoint hours + data processing ($/GB). For heavy egress patterns this is ~2-5x the raw data transfer of peering, but buys stronger isolation and simpler per-account controls.
- VPC Peering: low per-GB transfer (intra-region) and less per-hour overhead, but operational surface area grows (route tables, security groups, cross-account sharing). Not recommended if you explicitly want to avoid transitive routes.
- VPN: low fixed-hourly cost for AWS side tunnels + data transfer; can be constrained at high throughput and adds latency. Good as secondary/failover.

Configuration snippets to include in the writeup:
- NLB listeners (TCP 443), target group type = IP, health checks / 10s, deregistration delay 60s.
- Endpoint policy allowing only ACME account IDs to accept connections.
- BGP on VPN with advertisements limited to ACME on-prem CIDRs; keep routes specific.

Fallback & resilience:
- If PrivateLink NLB saturates in an AZ, traffic will rebalance to other AZs; ensure cross-zone enabled and autoscale NLB target capacity.
- Use DNS-based client retry/backoff and client-side connection pooling to avoid per-call connection churn.

I'll draft a 1-pager (attached) and a short rollout checklist. If you want, Jordan can sketch the NLB sizing math based on their traffic spreadsheet.

-Kimberly
Kimberly Park
Network Architect, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Tom Sullivan <tom.sullivan@acme.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-09-03T16:40:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Connecting Redwood Private - guidance request

Tom —

Thanks for the patience. Per Kimberly: our recommendation is to lead with PrivateLink (interface endpoints) for initial rollout and use an IPSec site-to-site VPN as a failover path for on-prem connectivity. Key reasons: preserves your non-transitive constraint, gives account-level isolation, and aligns best with SOC2 expectations.

Next steps we suggest:
1) Pilot: create a PrivateLink endpoint in one ACME account + region, deploy Redwood NLBs across AZs, run load tests at your peak profile (5k-10k req/s, simulate 200 MB/s bursts).
2) Validate monitoring: p99 latency, NLB target metrics, ENI counts; iterate on NLB target scaling and client connection handling.
3) Configure IPSec VPN tunnels (BGP) to Redwood for on-prem fallback; test failover and MTU.
4) If sustained high bandwidth shows up, plan Direct Connect for the long term.

I've attached a one-page comparison and the spreadsheet with our initial capacity assumptions. Jordan will follow up with NLB sizing numbers if you want a more granular cost estimate.

Would you like us to open a shared Confluence / runbook and schedule a 30m tech call with NetOps this week?

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Head of Solutions, Redwood Inference
Attachments: privatelink-vs-peering-onepager.pdf, acme-mapping-traffic-estimates.xlsx

messages:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Liam Ng <liam.ng@acme.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows

Hi Liam —

Thanks again for your time last week. We'd like to get the hands-on demo scheduled with your engineers, procurement, and security so we can walk through architecture, networking, and pricing. To make this workable across your EU/APAC/US stakeholders, we typically run a rotating weekly slot so no single region is always outside business hours.

Proposed approach:
- Two recurring 1hr sessions that alternate weekly: one timeslot optimized for APAC/EU overlap, the other for US/EU overlap.
- SE-led tech section (60–75 min) for VPC/network verification + live inference test.
- 15 min optional procurement/security mini-review on the first session in the series.

Please see the attached draft agenda (demo_agenda_v1.pdf). Can you share the right procurement and security contacts to loop in? I can set up a Doodle poll with 3 candidate windows; if you prefer a direct calendar invite we can do that as well.

Candidate windows (local):
- Option A (US-friendly): Thu 09:00–10:15 PT / Thu 17:00–18:15 BST / Fri 21:30–22:45 IST
- Option B (EU-friendly): Wed 16:00–17:15 BST / Wed 08:00–09:15 PT / Thu 20:30–21:45 IST

If those look reasonable I'll send the poll and loop in Avery for the tech checklist.

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0182

Attachments: demo_agenda_v1.pdf
From: Liam Ng <liam.ng@acme.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>, Sarah Kwan <sarah_procurement@acme.com>, Tanvi Patel <tanvi.patel@globalsec.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 16:40:00 +0100
Subject: Re: Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows

Thanks Jordan — this looks sensible. Ill coordinate internally and copy procurement/security. A few quick notes from our side:

- Procurement (Sarah) will need a short window to review vendor terms and PO flow; shell confirm timing below.
- Security (Tanvi) wants to see the network checklist and confirm whether a dedicated VPC peering test can be included in the session.
- For our engineers, rotating is ideal. Our team prefers:
  * APAC-friendly weeks: 08:30–09:45 SGT (works for our Bangalore office on IST offsets)
  * EU-friendly weeks: 16:00–17:15 BST (works for London and Amsterdam)
  * US-friendly mornings are OK once every 3 weeks; wed rather the sequence be EU/APAC/US so no one location gets the worst slot every week.

Ive looped Sarah and Tanvi to confirm availability and any vendor onboarding steps. If you can share a Doodle, thatll speed things up.

Best,
Liam Ng
Director, Platform Engineering — Acme Corp
liam.ng@acme.com
+44 20 7946 0123
From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Liam Ng <liam.ng@acme.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 19:05:00 +0100
Subject: Re: Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows

Quick technical notes — thanks for the agenda, Jordan.

- We should plan for 75 minutes for the first session (60 min demo + 15 min connectivity troubleshooting). Subsequent sessions can be 60 min if the VPC peering and KV cache are verified.
- Please include a test dataset and a short list of example prompts (we can demo latency distribution with your real traffic pattern).
- Im attaching a condensed network checklist (network_checklist.xlsx) that covers firewall rules, allowed IP ranges, and required ports for VPC peering. If Tanvi can confirm the security proxy setup ahead of time, we can avoid a lot of friction in the live test.

A couple of practical asks:
- Confirm whether the Acme environment allows outbound SSH to our test harness for a 10-minute connectivity check.
- If you have strict browser policies for meeting recordings, flag them now so we can plan to do a non-recorded live test if needed.

Thanks — Avery
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
avery.johnson@redwood.com
+1 (646) 555-0225

Attachments: network_checklist.xlsx
From: Sarah Kwan <sarah_procurement@acme.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Liam Ng <liam.ng@acme.com>
Cc: Tanvi Patel <tanvi.patel@globalsec.com>
Date: Thu, 03 Sep 2026 08:22:00 +0530
Subject: Re: Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows

Thanks for including procurement. We have a quick vendor intake and PO process: it typically takes 7 business days once we have the vendor form and an indicative SOW/estimate. Ive attached our vendor onboarding form (ACME_vendor_form.pdf) — please fill the red fields and return. Also, we need a short list of pricing buckets (per-token estimate, reserved capacity options) so we can route to finance.

For scheduling, the week of Sep 13 works for the first session from a procurement timeline perspective. If you can pencil in the Doodle options and include a calendar invite link, thatll help expedite approvals.

Cheers,
Sarah Kwan
Procurement Manager — Acme Corp
sarah_procurement@acme.com
+91 80 5555 2100

Based on the replies I propose we run a repeating series that alternates weekly between:
- Series A (EU-friendly): Wednesdays 16:00–17:15 BST (UTC+1) — good overlap for EU engineers and late US mornings.
- Series B (APAC-friendly): Thursdays 08:30–09:45 SGT / IST offsets for Bangalore — good overlap for APAC and early EU.

Ive added a calendar placeholder (https://calendar.google.com/event?eid=7gqexample) for the first session in the week of Sep 13; once the Doodle finishes Ill convert to an actual invite and include the SE runbook and the filled vendor form. HubSpot deal: https://hubspot.example/contacts/ACME-2026-RED-0047 (added prospect notes and expected timeline).

Avery — well plan 75 minutes for the first meeting and Ill ask Acme to send the test prompts and any IP allowlist if available.

Jordan

Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 02 Sep 2026 19:05:00 +0100
Subject: Re: Cross-region demo slotting & logistics - rotating weekly windows

Quick technical notes — thanks for the agenda, Jordan.

- We should plan for 75 minutes for the first session (60 min demo + 15 min connectivity troubleshooting). Subsequent sessions can be 60 min if the VPC peering and KV cache are verified.
- Please include a test dataset and a short list of example prompts (we can demo latency distribution with your real traffic pattern).
- Im attaching a condensed network checklist (network_checklist.xlsx) that covers firewall rules, allowed IP ranges, and required ports for VPC peering. If Tanvi can confirm the security proxy setup ahead of time, we can avoid a lot of friction in the live test.

A couple of practical asks:
- Confirm whether the Acme environment allows outbound SSH to our test harness for a 10-minute connectivity check.
- If you have strict browser policies for meeting recordings, flag them now so we can plan to do a non-recorded live test if needed.

Thanks — Avery

avery.johnson@redwood.com
+1 (646) 555-0225

Attachments: network_checklist.xlsx

source.subject: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing
messages:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Asha Menon <asha.menon@finsecure.com>
Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@finsecure.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing

Asha — great to meet you on Friday. Per our kickoff, I put together a suggested sequencing for access controls during onboarding so we don’t block the first-week integration work. High-level plan attached (redwood_onboarding_sequence_v1.pdf).

Quick summary:
- Week 1 (P0): SSO via SAML, basic role mappings, test users in staging.
- Week 2 (P1): SCIM provisioning for user sync + group mappings.
- Week 3 (P1): Service account pattern — ephemeral SA for CI/CD and a small set of long-lived read-only SA for monitoring.
- Week 4 (P2): IP allowlist for production endpoints, ops runbook and rollback.

Ask: can your security team confirm the IP range(s) we should request and whether SCIM will be enabled for all org units? If you prefer, I can loop in Monica and Jordan to draft the concrete actions and evidence pack for your SOC review.

Thanks,
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood_onboarding_sequence_v1.pdf (PDF)]

From: Asha Menon <asha.menon@finsecure.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@finsecure.com>, FinSecure Security <security@finsecure.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2026 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing

Ben — thanks for the clear plan and the attachment. Rajiv and I reviewed briefly; security wants to validate the service account model and see a mapping of which tokens will be long-lived vs ephemeral. Also, SOC asked for an evidence spreadsheet for: SAML metadata, SCIM endpoint ACLs, IP CIDR list, and service account key rotation policy.

Can you provide the evidence template? Also note: FinSecure requires IP ranges be narrowed to two CIDR blocks per vendor (we can expand later). Our SCIM rollout will start with HR and Engineering OUs only.

Cc'ing our security alias so they can add any must-have controls.

—Asha
Asha Menon
Head of Partnerships | FinSecure
[Inline: Rajiv suggests an architecture call Wed AM]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2026 14:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing

Forwarding FinSecure notes — they want an evidence spreadsheet and confirmation on SA rotation. Please prepare:
1) A one-page pattern doc that explains our ephemeral SA model (how token issuance/rotation works for ephemeral vs long-lived).
2) A prefilled evidence workbook matching their ask (SAML metadata, SCIM ACLs, IP CIDR, rotation logs). Use finsecure-evidence-request.xlsx as a template.
3) A concise checklist of actions we need from FinSecure (SAML metadata, two CIDR blocks, list of HR/Eng OU IDs).

Jordan — can you be on the Wed AM architecture call? Monica — I'll own the SOC evidence pack and coordinating responses. Neha — please confirm whether we can provide a 30-day log export for rotation events from our staging environment.

Thanks — I’m proposing Wed 09:30 PT for the call; Asha/Rajiv indicated availability. If that works I’ll send a calendar invite and include the preliminary evidence workbook.

Best,
Ben
[Attachment: finsecure-evidence-request.xlsx (Excel)]

From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
Cc: Asha Menon <asha.menon@finsecure.com>, Rajiv Singh <rajiv.singh@finsecure.com>, FinSecure Security <security@finsecure.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Sep 2026 14:45:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FinSecure onboarding: SSO/SCIM, ephemeral service accounts & IP allowlist sequencing

All — thanks for moving quickly. Confirming deliverables and timing: 
- Evidence workbook: I attached a prefilled sheet (tab per control) and notes. We can deliver the first pass by end of day Thu (2026-09-02).
- Ephemeral SA pattern doc: Jordan has sketched the flow (OAuth client creds + short TTL tokens + automated rotation). Jordan — please add an operations snippet showing how to revoke a token and how to request a short-lived SA for CI pipelines.
- Logs: Neha confirms we can export rotation events from staging for the prior 30 days and will export as CSV by Thu EOD.
- IP ranges: Asha/Rajiv — once you confirm the two CIDR blocks we’ll whitelist them in staging and cut a PR to production allowlist during the Week 4 window.

Proposed Wed architecture call (per Ben): Wed 09:30 PT / 12:30 ET — I’ll send the invite to everyone in this thread with the Zoom link and a shared doc. Agenda will be: 1) SCIM scope (HR + Eng OUs), 2) SA patterns & rotation, 3) IP allowlist process & rollback, 4) evidence pack walkthrough.

Action owners: 
- Monica: coordinate evidence pack and SOC touchpoints (owner)
- Jordan: SA pattern doc + ops snippets (owner)
- Neha: staging log export (owner)
- Ben: schedule call + follow FinSecure asks (owner)

Thanks — Monica Patel
Senior Customer Success Manager | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood_onboard_evidence_workbook_prefill.xlsx (Excel)]

source.subject: Re: Meadow Analytics MSA — Indemnity (defend vs. reimburse) and evidence/control
messages:
From: Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 30 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Hi Ben,

Thanks for taking the call earlier. Attached is Meadow's mark-up of the MSA and the specific order form excerpt that triggered procurement escalation. The primary sticking point for our legal team is the defend vs. reimburse language around third-party IP claims that may stem from model output. They want either a full defense obligation or a much higher cap + insurance carve-out.

Two quick clarifications from our side:
- Meadow will not accept an open-ended operational control carve (i.e., vendor retains full control without cooperation).
- Evidence preservation and chain-of-custody are critical for us — we need explicit timelines in the MSA for notice and technical artifact requests.

Please route to your legal contacts and let us know a window to discuss compromise language.

Attachments: Meadow_MSA_redline_20260830.docx (track changes), Redline_highlighted_comments.pdf

Best,
Tom
Procurement Lead, Meadow Analytics

On Sun, Aug 30, 2026 at 08:45 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> (internal notes: placeholder — original scheduling note moved into thread)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@meadow.com>, Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 30 Aug 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Elena / Tom — thanks for sending. Looping in Kimberly (legal) and Jordan (solutions) so we can respond with concrete compromise language.

High level from our side: Redwood is aligned on evidence timelines and preserving artifacts. Where we differ is allocation of defense control for third-party IP claims tied to model output. We typically propose:
- Redwood defends where we have primary operational control over the model (e.g., hosted model code and deployment), and we get to manage defense with cooperation from the customer.
- For customer-provided prompts/data or customer-hosted deployments, we prefer a reimburse model or limited obligation.

Kimberly — can you review Meadow's redline and propose a short set of options (1–2 paragraph clauses) we can present to procurement? I'd like to propose one option that keeps defense with Redwood plus a higher cap and a second option that moves to reimburse + insurance-first approach for customer-hosted variants.

Tom / Elena — is Meadow willing to accept a middle-ground where we: (a) accept defend for hosted models but add a $5M cap + insurance-first waterfall, and (b) for customer-hosted / BYO data, agree to reimburse with capped liability but include a defined evidence-preservation SOW and a 30-day notice window for technical artifacts?

Happy to jump on a 30-minute call tomorrow if that helps.

Thanks,
Ben
Account Exec — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: none in this reply.

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Elena Morales <elena@meadow.com>, Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>
Date: Mon, 31 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Ben / Jordan — I reviewed Meadow's redline and drafted two discrete proposal texts we can table with procurement. I've pasted them below so procurement can see precise language; if they prefer tracked changes, we can circulate a single redline doc.

Option A — Hosted-defense, insurance-first cap (preferred compromise):
"Redwood shall defend Customer against any third-party claim alleging that Redwood's hosted service (as provided by Redwood under this Agreement) directly infringes a third party's intellectual property rights, provided that (i) Customer notifies Redwood in writing promptly upon becoming aware of such claim, (ii) Customer grants Redwood sole control of the defense and settlement of such claim, and (iii) Redwood is provided reasonable cooperation and all relevant technical artifacts. Redwood's aggregate liability for indemnification under this Section shall be capped at $5,000,000. The Parties agree that any recovery under Customer's applicable insurance policies shall be applied before Redwood's indemnity cap is triggered (insurance-first waterfall)."

Option B — Reimburse for customer-controlled environments (BYO/On-Prem):
"To the extent any claim arises from Customer-provided models, customer datasets, or Customer-hosted deployments (including on-premises or VPC-managed deployments), Redwood's obligation shall be limited to reimbursement of documented defense costs and settlements directly attributable to Redwood's breach, subject to a cap of $1,000,000. Customer shall have the option to control the defense for Customer-controlled environments, provided Redwood may participate and seek reimbursement as set forth above. For these scenarios, the Parties will memorialize technical evidence collection timelines and chain-of-custody procedures in an attached Order Form SOW (Evidence SOW)."

Key operational asks to include in the MSA/Order Form:
- Notice window: Customer must give Redwood written notice within 30 days of becoming aware of a third-party claim; Redwood will have 10 business days to request technical artifacts necessary to evaluate the claim.
- Evidence SOW: Standard artifact list (conversation logs, prompt + prompt metadata, model version, inference logs, KV cache snapshot where possible) and custodian contact for production of artifacts within 15 business days.
- Subrogation: Redwood reserves the right to pursue subrogation against third parties responsible for the infringement where appropriate.

I recommend we present Option A for the hosted use-case and Option B for BYO/on-prem. If Meadow pushes for defend on all environments, propose a higher cap ($10M) and a specific carveout that excludes claims arising from Customer-supplied training data or prompts.

If Ben is comfortable, I can prepare a tracked-redline MSA showing Option A/B and a short Order Form SOW stub.

Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Contracts & Privacy
Redwood Inference

Attachments: none (draft text inline).

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@meadow.com>, Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>

Subject: Re: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Elena / Tom — appreciate the quick review. Kimberly and I discussed and below is a single package proposal to present to Meadow procurement / counsel tomorrow. We think this addresses Meadow's preservation & control needs while limiting Redwood's open-ended exposure.

Proposal (package):
1) Hosted deployments (Redwood-managed): Redwood will defend as per Option A with a $5M indemnity cap and an insurance-first waterfall. Redwood will have primary control of defense but will accept a process for customer objections to settlement if requested in writing (standard reasonableness test).
2) Customer-hosted / BYO: Reimbursement-only as per Option B with $1M cap and the Evidence SOW attached to the Order Form. Customer may elect to assume defense; Redwood may reimburse documented costs if Customer controls defense.

Elena — can you confirm tomorrow morning whether Meadow would accept this package or wants defend everywhere? If defend-everywhere, we can propose the $10M cap and insurance-first but will need procurement sign-off internally.

title: Re: Meadow Analytics MSA — Indemnity (defend vs. reimburse) and evidence/control
preview:
From: Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 30 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Hi Ben,

Thanks for taking the call earlier. Attached is Meadow's mark-up of the MSA and the specific order form excerpt that triggered procurement escalation. The primary sticking point for our legal team is the defend vs. reimburse language around third-party IP claims that may stem from model output. They want either a full defense obligation or a much higher cap + insurance carve-out.

Two quick clarifications from our side:
- Meadow will not accept an open-ended operational control carve (i.e., vendor retains full control without cooperation).
- Evidence preservation and chain-of-custody are critical for us — we need explicit timelines in the MSA for notice and technical artifact requests.

Please route to your legal contacts and let us know a window to discuss compromise language.

Attachments: Meadow_MSA_redline_20260830.docx (track changes), Redline_highlighted_comments.pdf

Best,
Tom
Procurement Lead, Meadow Analytics

On Sun, Aug 30, 2026 at 08:45 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> (internal notes: placeholder — original scheduling note moved into thread)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Morales <elena@meadow.com>, Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 30 Aug 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Elena / Tom — thanks for sending. Looping in Kimberly (legal) and Jordan (solutions) so we can respond with concrete compromise language.

High level from our side: Redwood is aligned on evidence timelines and preserving artifacts. Where we differ is allocation of defense control for third-party IP claims tied to model output. We typically propose:
- Redwood defends where we have primary operational control over the model (e.g., hosted model code and deployment), and we get to manage defense with cooperation from the customer.
- For customer-provided prompts/data or customer-hosted deployments, we prefer a reimburse model or limited obligation.

Kimberly — can you review Meadow's redline and propose a short set of options (1–2 paragraph clauses) we can present to procurement? I'd like to propose one option that keeps defense with Redwood plus a higher cap and a second option that moves to reimburse + insurance-first approach for customer-hosted variants.

Tom / Elena — is Meadow willing to accept a middle-ground where we: (a) accept defend for hosted models but add a $5M cap + insurance-first waterfall, and (b) for customer-hosted / BYO data, agree to reimburse with capped liability but include a defined evidence-preservation SOW and a 30-day notice window for technical artifacts?

Happy to jump on a 30-minute call tomorrow if that helps.

Thanks,
Ben
Account Exec — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: none in this reply.


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Elena Morales <elena@meadow.com>, Tom Briggs <tom.briggs@meadow.com>
Date: Mon, 31 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Meadow MSA redline — indemnity and defense concerns

Ben / Jordan — I reviewed Meadow's redline and drafted two discrete proposal texts we can table with procurement. I've pasted them below so procurement can see precise language; if they prefer tracked changes, we can circulate a single redline doc.

Option A — Hosted-defense, insurance-first cap (preferred compromise):
\"Redwood shall defend Customer against any third-party claim alleging that Redwood's hosted service (as provided by Redwood under this Agreement) directly infringes a third party's intellectual property rights, provided that (i) Customer notifies Redwood in writing promptly upon becoming aware of such claim, (ii) Customer grants Redwood sole control of the defense and settlement of such claim, and (iii) Redwood is provided reasonable cooperation and all relevant technical artifacts. Redwood's aggregate liability for indemnification under this Section shall be capped at $5,000,000. The Parties agree that any recovery under Customer's applicable insurance policies shall be applied before Redwood's indemnity cap is triggered (insurance-first waterfall).\"

Option B — Reimburse for customer-controlled environments (BYO/On-Prem):
\"To the extent any claim arises from Customer-provided models, customer datasets, or Customer-hosted deployments (including on-premises or VPC-managed deployments), Redwood's obligation shall be limited to reimbursement of documented defense costs and settlements directly attributable to Redwood's breach, subject to a cap of $1,000,000. Customer shall have the option to control the defense for Customer-controlled environments, provided Redwood may participate and seek reimbursement as set forth above. For these scenarios, the Parties will memorialize technical evidence collection timelines and chain-of-custody procedures in an attached Order Form SOW (Evidence SOW).\"

Key operational asks to include in the MSA/Order Form:
- Notice window: Customer must give Redwood written notice within 30 days of becoming aware of a third-party claim; Redwood will have 10 business days to request technical artifacts necessary to evaluate the claim.
- Evidence SOW: Standard artifact list (conversation logs, prompt + prompt metadata, model version, inference logs, KV cache snapshot where possible) and custodian contact for production of artifacts within 15 business days.
- Subrogation: Redwood reserves the right to pursue subrogation against third parties responsible for the infringement where appropriate.

I recommend we present Option A for the hosted use-case and Option B for BYO/on-prem. If Meadow pushes for defend on all environments, propose a higher cap ($10M) and a specific carveout that excludes claims arising from Customer-supplied training data or prompts.

If Ben is comfortable, I can prepare a tracked-redline MSA showing Option A/B and a short Order Form SOW stub.

Kimberly Park
Senior Counsel, Contracts & Privacy
Redwood Inference

Attachments: none (draft text inline).

source.subject: Private Offer: flexible bucket triggers, regional footing, and reseller fee flow
messages:
From: Carlos Mendez <carlos@fintechco.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@fintechco.com
Date: Fri, 21 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

Hi David / Jordan / Naomi,

Thanks again for the demo last week. FintechCo is close to a procurement window but we need clarity on three items before we can finalize the preferred partner list:

1) Bucketization and triggers — we have a seasonal burst profile (Q4 spikes). We want a private-offer config that can slide between two bucket sizes with an automatic fallback if usage exceeds the top bucket. Is that something Redwood can express on the PO?

2) Regional list price differences — our customers are spread across US regions and APAC; for now we need US-region behavior documented (list-price parity vs per-region list).

3) Reseller/partner fee passback — LocalCloud (their reseller) needs a consistent % fee carved from the final invoice path. Procurement requires an example waterfall to show auditors.

Attached is our RFP (FintechCo_RFP_v2.pdf) with a short section on invoicing rules. Can you share a sample private-offer configuration and an ops playbook snippet that shows invoice routing and a simple failover policy?

Proposed next step: 30min call early next week to align on an example config.

Carlos Mendez
Head of Platform Partnerships
FintechCo
Attachment: FintechCo_RFP_v2.pdf (application/pdf)

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Carlos Mendez <carlos@fintechco.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 22 Aug 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

Carlos — thanks for the clear ask and the RFP. Short answers up front, then a proposed deliverable and timing.

Quick answers:
- Yes, we can model tiered buckets with a graceful overflow/fallback to a higher-capacity variant. That is implemented as two private-offer SKUs with automated routing and a configured fallback policy (switch at 95% bucket utilization).
- Regionally we support per-region list prices on private offers; for your auditors we can produce an example waterfall showing list -> discount -> reseller passback. For the US pilot we'll propose a single-US-list with regional billing attributes.
- Reseller fee: we can passback a fixed-percent on the invoice path and show the waterfall. We generally recommend doing reseller passback as a post-billing allocation (example waterfall attached).

Deliverable: I will send a draft private-offer configuration (spreadsheet) that includes: SKU names, bucket sizes, overflow routing rules, and an example invoice waterfall showing LocalCloud fee. Irene will add the ops playbook excerpt describing metering and how the 95% trigger escalates to the higher-capacity SKU.

Proposed timeline: draft config attached by Monday EOD (we aim for 2026-08-24). If that works, can we lock a 30-minute call Tues 8/25 at 10am PT?

Next steps for you: confirm the reseller % you expect (example: 8% passback) and confirm whether LocalCloud will need the reseller fee visible on the customer invoice or as a separate settlement.

Thanks — looping in Irene for the ops details.

—
David Kim
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
Attachment: draft-privoffer-config.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Cc: Carlos Mendez <carlos@fintechco.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 24 Aug 2026 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

David / Carlos —
Quick confirmation from my side: I will validate the invoice routing path with Marketplace ops (AWS partner flow) and confirm whether the reseller fee must be a listed line item or can be a separate settlement.

Also David — can you confirm the deal_id you want us to use for wiring the draft? I’ll sync with finance once we have that.

– Jordan
Principal Deal Ops, Redwood Inference

From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
To: Carlos Mendez <carlos@fintechco.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Aug 2026 14:10:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

Carlos / team — looping in ops details and a few constraints so expectations are aligned: 

1) Bucket overflow behavior: we implement as two linked SKUs (primary bucket + overflow SKU). Metering is continuous and the switch is event-driven (we monitor KV cache pressure + token consumption). The trigger we usually recommend is a 95% consumption window over any 10-minute sliding interval. At that point requests are routed to the overflow SKU for new sessions; in-flight sessions continue on primary to avoid tail latency spikes.

2) Billing / invoice waterfall: marketplace-broker invoices (AWS) will list our settlement lines; reseller % passback can live either as a line item on the invoice (requires partner-visible listing) or as separate settlement. If LocalCloud requires audit visibility we recommend line-item passback. Example is in the ops_playbook attachment.

3) Failure mode / fallback: if overflow SKU capacity is exhausted (rare), we have an automated fallback chain: a) route to reserved capacity account (if you have it), b) fall back to a lower-latency small model variant with notification header, c) last-resort rate-limited queue with 429 cadence. We log and surface all fallback events to the Console + auditors.

4) Procurement evidence: we can produce a short export showing simulated monthly invoicing with the waterfall and passback allocations for your auditors.

I attached a trimmed ops playbook excerpt that shows the trigger math and the recommended listener/webhook for overflow alerts. Two small asks: confirm 1) whether LocalCloud needs the fee on the customer-facing invoice and 2) the reseller percent.

Cheers,
Irene Choi
Sr. Product Ops — Redwood Inference
Attachment: ops_playbook.md (text/markdown)

On Sat, Aug 22, 2026 at 11:03 AM David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com> wrote:
> Carlos — thanks for the clear ask and the RFP. Short answers up front, then a proposed deliverable and timing.
> 
> (quoted earlier message omitted)

From: Carlos Mendez <carlos@fintechco.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, procurement@fintechco.com
Date: Fri, 27 Aug 2026 15:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

Thanks — this is really helpful. A few clarifications and next steps: 

- LocalCloud would like the reseller fee visible on the customer invoice (audit requirement). We are asking for an 8% passback as our baseline — can you show that in the example waterfall?
- We want the overflow trigger at 95% with the in-flight session behavior you described. Good to see the fallback chain; please include the 429 rate-limited queue behavior in the docs.

Can you send the draft spreadsheet and ops excerpt as promised? If it looks good we will surface the example to procurement and then request a templated PO.

I’ll take the 30-minute call — Tue 8/25 at 10am PT works for me, but we are one week behind in the thread dates (your draft timeline was 8/24). If you can send the draft by EOD Monday 8/24 we will be ready for the Tuesday sync.

Finally, please send a DocuSign-ready MA once we agree the config so our procurement can fast-track signature.

Carlos Mendez
Head of Platform Partnerships
FintechCo

> Carlos / team — looping in ops details and a few constraints so expectations are aligned: 
> 
> (quoted earlier message omitted)
