Date: Tue, 24 Aug 2026 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

David / Carlos —
Quick confirmation from my side: I will validate the invoice routing path with Marketplace ops (AWS partner flow) and confirm whether the reseller fee must be a listed line item or can be a separate settlement.

Also David — can you confirm the deal_id you want us to use for wiring the draft? I’ll sync with finance once we have that.

– Jordan
Principal Deal Ops, Redwood Inference

From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
To: Carlos Mendez <carlos@fintechco.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 26 Aug 2026 14:10:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

Carlos / team — looping in ops details and a few constraints so expectations are aligned: 

1) Bucket overflow behavior: we implement as two linked SKUs (primary bucket + overflow SKU). Metering is continuous and the switch is event-driven (we monitor KV cache pressure + token consumption). The trigger we usually recommend is a 95% consumption window over any 10-minute sliding interval. At that point requests are routed to the overflow SKU for new sessions; in-flight sessions continue on primary to avoid tail latency spikes.

2) Billing / invoice waterfall: marketplace-broker invoices (AWS) will list our settlement lines; reseller % passback can live either as a line item on the invoice (requires partner-visible listing) or as separate settlement. If LocalCloud requires audit visibility we recommend line-item passback. Example is in the ops_playbook attachment.

3) Failure mode / fallback: if overflow SKU capacity is exhausted (rare), we have an automated fallback chain: a) route to reserved capacity account (if you have it), b) fall back to a lower-latency small model variant with notification header, c) last-resort rate-limited queue with 429 cadence. We log and surface all fallback events to the Console + auditors.

4) Procurement evidence: we can produce a short export showing simulated monthly invoicing with the waterfall and passback allocations for your auditors.

I attached a trimmed ops playbook excerpt that shows the trigger math and the recommended listener/webhook for overflow alerts. Two small asks: confirm 1) whether LocalCloud needs the fee on the customer-facing invoice and 2) the reseller percent.

Cheers,
Irene Choi
Sr. Product Ops — Redwood Inference
Attachment: ops_playbook.md (text/markdown)

To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>

Date: Fri, 21 Aug 2026 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

Hi David / Jordan / Naomi,

Thanks again for the demo last week. FintechCo is close to a procurement window but we need clarity on three items before we can finalize the preferred partner list:

1) Bucketization and triggers — we have a seasonal burst profile (Q4 spikes). We want a private-offer config that can slide between two bucket sizes with an automatic fallback if usage exceeds the top bucket. Is that something Redwood can express on the PO?

2) Regional list price differences — our customers are spread across US regions and APAC; for now we need US-region behavior documented (list-price parity vs per-region list).

3) Reseller/partner fee passback — LocalCloud (their reseller) needs a consistent % fee carved from the final invoice path. Procurement requires an example waterfall to show auditors.

Attached is our RFP (FintechCo_RFP_v2.pdf) with a short section on invoicing rules. Can you share a sample private-offer configuration and an ops playbook snippet that shows invoice routing and a simple failover policy?

Proposed next step: 30min call early next week to align on an example config.

Carlos Mendez
Head of Platform Partnerships
FintechCo
Attachment: FintechCo_RFP_v2.pdf (application/pdf)

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Carlos Mendez <carlos@fintechco.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 22 Aug 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Private Offer ask — flexible buckets, geo list pricing, reseller fee flow

Carlos — thanks for the clear ask and the RFP. Short answers up front, then a proposed deliverable and timing.

Quick answers:
- Yes, we can model tiered buckets with a graceful overflow/fallback to a higher-capacity variant. That is implemented as two private-offer SKUs with automated routing and a configured fallback policy (switch at 95% bucket utilization).
- Regionally we support per-region list prices on private offers; for your auditors we can produce an example waterfall showing list -> discount -> reseller passback. For the US pilot we'll propose a single-US-list with regional billing attributes.
- Reseller fee: we can passback a fixed-percent on the invoice path and show the waterfall. We generally recommend doing reseller passback as a post-billing allocation (example waterfall attached).

Deliverable: I will send a draft private-offer configuration (spreadsheet) that includes: SKU names, bucket sizes, overflow routing rules, and an example invoice waterfall showing LocalCloud fee. Irene will add the ops playbook excerpt describing metering and how the 95% trigger escalates to the higher-capacity SKU.

Proposed timeline: draft config attached by Monday EOD (we aim for 2026-08-24). If that works, can we lock a 30-minute call Tues 8/25 at 10am PT?

Next steps for you: confirm the reseller % you expect (example: 8% passback) and confirm whether LocalCloud will need the reseller fee visible on the customer invoice or as a separate settlement.

Thanks — looping in Irene for the ops details.

—
David Kim
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
Attachment: draft-privoffer-config.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)

messages:
From: Maria Ortega <maria.ortega@fintegrate.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Security Team <security@fintegrate.com>, DevOps <devops@fintegrate.com>
Date: Wed, 26 Aug 2026 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: SOC2 / pen test / DPA — seeking a short evening window

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. We need to get a combined intake call to cover:
- SOC 2 scope and evidence handoff
- Pen-test scoping and vendor expectations
- DPA questions (legal will want to be part of the conversation)

Our engineering/security team is mostly booked during US business hours the week of Sep 6–10. Do you have any evening windows (US PT) we could propose for a single 60-minute session? If not, we can split across two 30-min blocks (security first, then legal).

Attendees on our side: Maria Ortega (security lead), Sam Patel (DevOps), and Legal (TBD).

Thanks — appreciate any compact options so we can lock a time for our pen vendor as well.

Best,
Maria Ortega
Security Lead, Fintegrate
attachments: none
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Maria Ortega <maria.ortega@fintegrate.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 26 Aug 2026 11:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SOC2 / pen test / DPA — seeking a short evening window

Maria —

Thanks for the note and the context. We can absolutely accommodate an evening slot and I think a single 60-minute session will be most efficient (we'll come with the intake docs and a rough runbook). A few compact options (US PT):

- Tue Sep 7, 18:00–19:00 PT
- Wed Sep 8, 19:30–20:30 PT (works well for your east coast folks)
- Thu Sep 9, 17:30–18:30 PT

If you prefer splitting, we can do Security (30m) + DPA/legal (30m) back-to-back on the same evening — we'll note that on the invite.

I've attached a short Walkthrough_Agenda_v2.pdf and the PenTest_Scoping_Form.xlsx to speed the intake. Lauren (cc'd) will own DPA questions and can review redlines ahead of the call — looping Jordan for capacity if we need extra engineering on the bridge.

Let me know which of the three slots is best and I will send a calendar invite with a short agenda and a link to a shared drive for evidence transfer. If none work, tell me your top two evening availabilities and I'll match.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
attachments: Walkthrough_Agenda_v2.pdf; PenTest_Scoping_Form.xlsx
From: Maria Ortega <maria.ortega@fintegrate.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Security Team <security@fintegrate.com>, DevOps <devops@fintegrate.com>
Date: Wed, 26 Aug 2026 14:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SOC2 / pen test / DPA — seeking a short evening window

On Wed, Aug 26, 2026 at 11:18 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
> Maria —
> 
> Thanks for the note and the context. We can absolutely accommodate an evening slot...

Ben — thanks for the options and the forms. Wed Sep 8, 19:30–20:30 PT works best for our group. We'll aim to have someone from legal on the tail end.

A couple asks before the call please:
- Can you confirm the expected attendee list from Redwood (names/roles)?
- Any pre-read you want our legal team to review (we have a 24–48 hour turnaround for redline comments)?
- For the pen vendor: do you have an example scope template we can share with them?

We'll slot Wed Sep 8 on our side tentatively and I'll confirm legal once I have their availability.

Thanks — Maria

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Maria Ortega <maria.ortega@fintegrate.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 27 Aug 2026 14:20:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SOC2 / pen test / DPA — seeking a short evening window

Maria — perfect, Wed Sep 8 19:30–20:30 PT is confirmed on our end. I've just sent a calendar invite (please accept when convenient).

Attendees from Redwood (confirmed):
- Ben Carter — Solutions (host)
- Lauren Bishop — Legal / Privacy (DPA redlines)
- Jordan Blake — Escalation / Technical lead (on-call if we need deeper infra details)
- Optional: Product/Runtime engineer (will join if we hit model-serving specifics)

Pre-reads and links:
- Walkthrough agenda (attached): Walkthrough_Agenda_v2.pdf
- Pen test scoping template: PenTest_Scoping_Form.xlsx (attached) — feel free to share with your vendor; it includes the default surface area and typical timing expectations.
- DPA redline draft: DPA_Redlined_v1.pdf (Lauren will review live during the call).
- Shared evidence folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-walkthrough-agenda
- HubSpot record: HS-45291 (for reference on provisioning and entitlement)

Logistics: we'll use the standard Redwood bridge in the calendar invite. If legal needs more time to redline ahead of the call, we can shorten the walkthrough to 45 minutes and leave 15 minutes for legal Q&A. Also open to splitting into two 30-minute blocks if your vendor needs a dedicated scoping slot.

If anything changes on your side (legal can't make it, or the pen vendor requests an earlier scoping chat), loop me and Lauren and we'll rework quickly. Looking forward to it.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
attachments: Walkthrough_Agenda_v2.pdf; PenTest_Scoping_Form.xlsx; DPA_Redlined_v1.pdf

- Walkthrough agenda (attached): Walkthrough_Agenda_v2.pdf
- Pen test scoping template: PenTest_Scoping_Form.xlsx (attached) — feel free to share with your vendor; it includes the default surface area and typical timing expectations.
- DPA redline draft: DPA_Redlined_v1.pdf (Lauren will review live during the call).
- Shared evidence folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-walkthrough-agenda
- HubSpot record: HS-45291 (for reference on provisioning and entitlement)

Logistics: we'll use the standard Redwood bridge in the calendar invite. If legal needs more time to redline ahead of the call, we can shorten the walkthrough to 45 minutes and leave 15 minutes for legal Q&A. Also open to splitting into two 30-minute blocks if your vendor needs a dedicated scoping slot.

If anything changes on your side (legal can't make it, or the pen vendor requests an earlier scoping chat), loop me and Lauren and we'll rework quickly. Looking forward to it.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
attachments: Walkthrough_Agenda_v2.pdf; PenTest_Scoping_Form.xlsx; DPA_Redlined_v1.pdf

source.subject: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC
messages:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@nexview.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 14:12:00 -0400
Subject: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC

Hi Daniel / Elena —

Thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed owners so we have a single source of truth for the POC plan. High level we heard:

- Goal: Validate real-time summarization + ranked insights for clinical notes (latency target < 200ms for tail, throughput ~120 req/s during peak).
- Primary constraints: PHI-handling requirements, VPC-only traffic, and a desire to run embeddings & reranker as part of the pipeline.

Proposed technical checklist and owners (please confirm):

1) SDK & auth setup (owner: Jordan)
   - Setup service account with scoped API keys and VPC egress rules. Evaluate short-lived tokens vs. static key + KMS rotation.
2) Routing & model variant policy (owner: Rishi)
   - Route high-priority traffic to a low-latency variant; failover to cost-optimized model on saturation.
3) Embeddings + reranker (owner: NexView + Redwood eng)
   - Store embeddings in your existing vector DB (we’ll provide embedding profiles).
4) Structured output / tool-calling (owner: Ava)
   - Define tool schema for lookup calls (patient lookup, medication mapping). We recommend explicit schema + strict type checking to reduce hallucination.
5) Prompt governance & safety (owner: Marissa + NexView Sec)
   - Versioned system prompts, injection mitigations (context window filters, whitelist/blacklist of tools), and a red-team plan.

Attachments: I’ve attached a one-page SOW and a first-pass architecture surface for discussion. Also included a small set of starter prompt templates (prompt-templates-v0.2.md).

Next steps / proposed timeline:
- Jordan: send SDK onboarding steps and credential template by EOD Thu (20 Aug).
- Rishi: share model routing policy examples and perf targets by Fri (21 Aug).
- NexView: provide sample anonymized notes + expected QPS by Mon (24 Aug).
- Marissa & Ava: organize a red-team kickoff for prompt injection tests (week of 8/31).

If anything above is off, flag it here. Otherwise I’ll update the HubSpot record and drive folder to reflect these owners.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Solutions Lead, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0192

Attachment: nexview-integration-sow-v1.pdf (PDF, 1 page); arch-surface-plan.drawio.png (PNG); prompt-templates-v0.2.md (MD)

From: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena.ruiz@nexview.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 16:03:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC

Thanks Ava — this is excellent and aligns with what we discussed. A few clarifications from our side: 

- For PHI, our security team requires token rotation and HSM-backed keys for any persistent secrets. Short-lived tokens with a refresh flow are preferred.
- Vector DB: we plan to use our existing FAISS cluster behind the VPN. Can Redwood’s embeddings profile be normalized to match our F32 layout (we currently store 1536-d floats)?
- Tooling: we like the explicit schema approach. For the POC we only need patient lookup and a simple ICD mapping tool.

We’ll upload a small anonymized dataset by Monday (24 Aug). I attached a quick doc that lists the PHI handling checklist from our security review.

Two quick asks:
1) Can Jordan provide the SDK quickstart that shows service-account creation and least-privilege roles?
2) Can Rishi share any example routing JSON for low-latency vs. cost-fallback so our infra team can pre-approve network policies?

Appreciate it —
Daniel Park
Head of Data Platform, NexView Analytics
Daniel.Park@nexview.ai | +1 (646) 212-3300

Attachment: nexview-security-checklist.pdf (PDF, 2 pages)

From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 19 Aug 2026 11:28:00 -0400
Subject: SDK onboarding & credential patterns

Hi Daniel —

I put together a short SDK quickstart and credential template and dropped it into the shared Drive (link below). Highlights:

- We support two onboarding flows: service account (recommended for VPC/private deployments) and short-lived OAuth tokens (recommended for hosted, low-friction demos).
- For your PHI requirement I recommend service account + KMS-wrapped API key with a 24-hour rotation window. We can integrate with your HSM for signing if needed.
- Example Terraform snippets included for binding least-privilege roles and creating VPC egress rules.

Quickstart link: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2bNexViewPOC (README: SDK-quickstart.md)

I’ll also attach a JSON example of a routing policy; Rishi — tagging you for the model variant perf numbers.

—Jordan
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Jordan.Blake@redwood.com

Attachment: SDK-quickstart.md (MD); credential-template.json (JSON)

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 09:15:00 -0400
Subject: Re: SDK onboarding & credential patterns / routing examples

Folks —

Posting an example routing policy here (abbreviated) and the perf budget we used in other clinical POCs. Full JSON is in the Drive.

- Low-latency route: model_variant: redwood-llama-13b-rt
  - Target p99: 180ms (tokenization included)
  - Batching: continuous batch window 5ms, max batch 16
- Cost-fallback route: model_variant: redwood-13b-opt
  - Switch when queue length > 60 or median latency > 250ms

Embedding profile: we export 1536-d float32 vectors by default. We can add a normalization flag to match your storage layout.

Prompt safety notes (quick):
- System prompts: store in versioned namespace (prompt:v0.1, prompt:v0.2). Each change must reference a changelog entry and approval from security.
- Injection mitigations: context sanitizers (strip user-injected tool calls), tool whitelist enforcement in the runtime, and per-request policy tags to disable unknown tools.
- Red-team plan: two-week focused attack window post-POC where we will run adversarial prompts, then produce a remediation backlog.

Next asks:
- Daniel / Elena: confirm vector format (1536 float32 ok?) and provide sample throughput profile.
- Marissa: I’ll sync with you on the prompt versioning workflow — do you prefer Git-based prompts or a UI in Console?

Thanks,
Rishi
Staff Engineer, Inference Runtime, Redwood Inference

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <daniel.park@nexview.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 22:03:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Next steps: integration & prompt safety for NexView POC

Thanks everyone — solid progress. A couple of governance items before we lock the POC scope: 

1) Prompt versioning: we prefer a Git-backed flow with signed commits for production system prompts. For the POC, we can accept a staged Console UI (with audit logs) but production must be Git-first.
2) Injection testing: Redwood will run an initial red-team pass, but NexView security must approve the test plan. We’ll share the attack vectors doc and proposed cadence by Friday.
3) Acceptance criteria:
   - No high-severity hallucinations on the sample dataset (as defined in our eval rubric).
   - Embedding cosine recall > 0.78 for top-5 retrievals on anonymized test set.

Action items recap (owners and due dates):
- Jordan: SDK quickstart + credential template — Thu 20 Aug (done).
- Rishi: routing JSON + perf targets — Fri 21 Aug (drive link).
- NexView (Daniel): sample anonymized notes + throughput profile — Mon 24 Aug.
- Marissa + NexView Sec: review red-team test plan — Fri 21 Aug.

I’ll update the HubSpot deal and schedule the red-team kickoff once the sample dataset lands.

—Marissa Cole
Enterprise Sales Manager, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

On Wed, Aug 18, 2026 at 2:12 PM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Daniel / Elena —
> 
> Thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed owners so we have a single source of truth for the POC plan. High level we heard:
> 
> - Goal: Validate real-time summarization + ranked insights for clinical notes (latency target < 200ms for tail, throughput ~120 req/s during peak).
> - Primary constraints: PHI-handling requirements, VPC-only traffic, and a desire to run embeddings & reranker as part of the pipeline.
> 
> Proposed technical checklist and owners (please confirm):
> 
> 1) SDK & auth setup (owner: Jordan)
> 2) Routing & model variant policy (owner: Rishi)
> 3) Embeddings + reranker (owner: NexView + Redwood eng)
> 4) Structured output / tool-calling (owner: Ava)
> 5) Prompt governance & safety (owner: Marissa + NexView Sec)
> 
> Attachment: nexview-integration-sow-v1.pdf (PDF, 1 page); arch-surface-plan.drawio.png (PNG); prompt-templates-v0.2.md (MD)

Action items recap (owners and due dates):
- Jordan: SDK quickstart + credential template — Thu 20 Aug (done).
- Rishi: routing JSON + perf targets — Fri 21 Aug (drive link).
- NexView (Daniel): sample anonymized notes + throughput profile — Mon 24 Aug.
- Marissa + NexView Sec: review red-team test plan — Fri 21 Aug.

I’ll update the HubSpot deal and schedule the red-team kickoff once the sample dataset lands.

—Marissa Cole
Enterprise Sales Manager, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

On Wed, Aug 18, 2026 at 2:12 PM Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Daniel / Elena —
> 
> Thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed owners so we have a single source of truth for the POC plan. High level we heard:
> 
> - Goal: Validate real-time summarization + ranked insights for clinical notes (latency target < 200ms for tail, throughput ~120 req/s during peak).
> - Primary constraints: PHI-handling requirements, VPC-only traffic, and a desire to run embeddings & reranker as part of the pipeline.
> 
> Proposed technical checklist and owners (please confirm):
> 
> 1) SDK & auth setup (owner: Jordan)
> 2) Routing & model variant policy (owner: Rishi)
> 3) Embeddings + reranker (owner: NexView + Redwood eng)
> 4) Structured output / tool-calling (owner: Ava)
> 5) Prompt governance & safety (owner: Marissa + NexView Sec)
> 
> Attachment: nexview-integration-sow-v1.pdf (PDF, 1 page); arch-surface-plan.drawio.png (PNG); prompt-templates-v0.2.md (MD)

source.subject: Regional econ snapshot + next steps for FinChain residency decision
source.messages:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emma Liu <emma.liu@finchain.com>
Cc: Andre Sousa <andre.sousa@finchain.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 14 Aug 2026 17:12:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: regional econ snapshot + a quick calc

Emma / Andre —

Thanks again for the call today. Summarizing the key points and a very compact cost snapshot so we can align on a feasible pilot:

- Current target workload: 4–6M combined prompt+completion tokens / month (mostly short-form inference, 80% chat-style prompts).
- Desired target price band: ~$250–$350 per 1M tokens (goal: predictable $/1M for budgeting).
- Residency requirement: EU data residency for EU users (~15% traffic), with the remainder US. EU residency is a requirement for the upcoming Q4 launch.

Quick back-of-envelope (list prices, pre-discount):
- US hosted baseline: $250 / 1M tokens
- EU hosted premium: +20% => $300 / 1M tokens
- If effective cache/hit-rate optimizations apply (we typically see 15–30% token reduction on query-heavy apps), you can drop net spend by roughly the same percentage.

Example monthly scenarios (no discounts, no caching):
- 4M tokens: US = $1,000 / month; EU portion (600k tokens @ $300/1M) adds $180 -> blended ~$1,180
- 6M tokens: US = $1,500; EU portion adds $270 -> blended ~$1,770

Attachments: I added a short spreadsheet with scenario rows and a one-pager that outlines hosted vs dedicated economics and where reserved capacity makes sense.

Next steps I propose:
1) You confirm a firm pilot volume (e.g., 3mo at 5M/mo).
2) We run a quick technical checklist (latency SLOs, KV cache feasibility for your prompt patterns).
3) We provision a 30-day pilot quote that shows both US-only and US+EU pricing (with clear per-1M tokens line items).

Happy to jump on a quick 20-minute call to walk the sheet if that helps.

Best,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: regional-unit-econ-sheet.xlsx (spreadsheet)]
[Attachment: redwood-pricing-compact.pdf (one-page PDF)]

-----Original Message-----
From: Emma Liu <emma.liu@finchain.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Andre Sousa <andre.sousa@finchain.com>
Date: Mon, 17 Aug 2026 09:30:00 +0100
Subject: Re: Follow-up: regional econ snapshot + a quick calc

Ben —

Thanks for this — the calc format is helpful. Couple of clarifying items from our side:
1) Confirmed pilot target: leaning toward 5M tokens/month (we want a 90-day window to validate usage).
2) Budget: we have an initial target of $1,400–$2,200 / month for inference (this is a draft allocation from the platform budget).
3) Residency split: roughly 15% of requests must remain in EU jurisdiction; the rest can be US.

A few quick questions:
- What does the EU premium buy us operationally? Is it strictly region hosting, or are there different isolation/contractual terms?
- If we hit 20M tokens / month after Q1 next year, how do tiered unit prices typically change?

If you can drop a few scenarios at the 5M target with our budget in mind that would be great. Also copy our infra lead Andre — he'll want the latency and KV cache notes.

Emma Liu
Head of Platform | FinChain
[Signature]

-----Original Message-----
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emma Liu <emma.liu@finchain.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 18 Aug 2026 11:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Follow-up: regional econ snapshot + a quick calc

Emma — great, thanks for the clarity. Two quick answers and a scenario table inline: 

EU premium — what it covers:
- Physical region hosting + data residency (data written to disk and logs stay in the selected EU region).
- Contractual assurances for residency (we flag the EU tenancy on the invoice and on the contract appendix); available KMS/HSM integrations by region.
- Operational delta: slightly higher infra cost and inter-region network egress that drives the ~15–25% premium. No functional difference in the runtime.

Tiered unit price examples (list -> illustrative net):
- 1–5M / month: US list $250 / 1M; EU list $300 / 1M
- 5–20M / month: US list $215 / 1M; EU list $258 / 1M (we typically can push the per-1M lower with committed volume)
- 20M+ / month: US list $180 / 1M; EU list $216 / 1M

5M pilot scenarios (no discounts, then illustrative 20% commit discount):
- No discount, no caching: blended (85% US / 15% EU) = (4.25M*$250 + 0.75M*$300) / 5 = blended ~$262 / 1M => $1,310 / month
- With optimistic 20% reserved-commit discount: blended ~$210 / 1M => $1,050 / month
- With 20% caching reduction on tokens + 20% commit discount: net spend ~ $840 / month

So your stated budget band ($1,400–$2,200) maps comfortably to a 5M pilot under list pricing, and gives runway for both dedicated/reserved options if you want lower net unit cost.

Recommended next steps:
- Ben/Jordan: prepare a 30-day pilot quote with both hosted US-only and hosted US+EU (line items showing list and a 20% committed discount scenario).
- Technical: Marissa to run the quick KV cache feasibility checklist with Andre (I attached a short checklist file).

I can also draft a suggested commercial (12-mo commit with quarterly true-ups) if you want to see the committed math.

Best,
Ben

[Attachment: eu-residency-cost-notes.txt (plain text)]

-----Original Message: Internal note -----
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 20 Aug 2026 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: FinChain pilot options & proposed commercial approach

Quick internal summary — do not forward to customer: 
- Customer: FinChain (Emma / Andre). Pilot: 5M/mo for 90 days, 15% EU residency.
- Recommended commercial: offer two lanes -> (A) Hosted pilot (no commit) at list; (B) 3-month pilot with optional 12-mo commit that converts to a reserved capacity credit with a 20–30% blended discount at signing. Aim to get a 12-mo commit for lower CPI if they agree to projected growth.
- Pricing math to include: per-1M list (US/EU), reserve discount scenarios (15/20/30%), and an explicit row for expected caching uplift (conservative 15%, optimistic 30%).

Action items:
- Jordan: build the pilot quote (line-itemized by region, show both list and -20% commit).
- Marissa: start technical checklist with Andre (latency targets, KV cache feasibility, and any PII data flow notes for EU residency).
- I will propose the commercial term language and guardrails for stateful caching (no residency drift).

Thanks — Ben

source.subject: International data residency commitments and DPA annex clarifications — Meridian Biosciences RFI
messages:
From: Claire Evans <claire.evans@meridianbio.com>
To: rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com
Cc: procurement@meridianbio.com, tom.liu@meridianbio.com
Date: 2026-08-17T08:12:00-04:00
Subject: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex Qs

Hi Rafael,

Thanks for taking our RFI. We have a few specific clarifications requested for Section 3 (attached workbook 'Meridian_RFI_Section3.xlsx'). Can you confirm the following at a minimum for the RFP response:

1) Do you offer deterministic routing guarantees for EU-origin requests (i.e., ability to keep processing and model weights within EU regions only)? If so, what contractual commitment can be included in the DPA annex and what operational controls enforce it?

2) If EU routing is requested but capacity in the EU cluster is constrained, what is the documented fallback behavior? Are customers notified and is there an opt-out to block fallback to non-EU processing?

3) Please provide an up-to-date subprocessor matrix indicating which subprocessors may store or process request data and their regions.

4) For data-protection-framework (DPF) evidence: can you provide sample logs/audit artifacts that show regional routing decisions, egress destinations, and a redacted example of a model-serving audit trail?

5) We'd like to see a suggested DPA annex table showing clause mappings for cross-border safeguards and where we can add a Meridian-specific residency paragraph.

Can you get back by end of week? We're consolidating answers for our legal and procurement teams. Happy to set a short call if helpful.

Best,
Claire Evans
Head of IT Procurement
Meridian Biosciences

Attachments: Meridian_RFI_Section3.xlsx (workbook)
From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com, jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com, neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com
Date: 2026-08-17T09:05:00-04:00
Subject: Fwd: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex Qs

Team — forwarding Meridian's Section 3 questions. Key asks are deterministic routing, fallback policy, subprocessor list, DPF evidence, and a DPA annex mapping.

Quick notes:
- We already have a routing control we can surface; needs legal language to turn into a DPA paragraph.
- Fallback options: we can describe policy + opt-out capability, but need to confirm notification SLA and whether we can offer hard-block for an enterprise contract.
- Neha, can you generate the subprocessor_matrix.csv with residency columns and last-processed timestamps?
- Kimberly, please draft the DPAAnnex table (start from our template) and include a clause we can offer for Meridian-specific residency commitments.
- Jordan, please prepare the sample audit artifacts we can safely share (redacted).

I'll collate and send to Claire. Target: send initial consolidated answer by EOD 8/17.

Rafael
Director, Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

[Forwarded message attached: Meridian_RFI_Section3.xlsx]
From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com, jordan.blake@jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com, neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com
Date: 2026-08-17T11:20:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex Qs

Rafael — drafting here. Attachments I'm preparing: DPF_Routing_SLA_draft.docx (text we can include in DPA annex) and subprocessor_matrix.csv (latest). Jordan — can you produce the redacted audit trail by 16:00 ET? Neha — will you confirm whether we can offer a contractual hard-block (no fallback) or only a best-effort notification + opt-out process?

Summary of proposed answers (high-level):
- Deterministic routing: Yes. We offer region-pin routing at the tenant level (customer can require EU-only processing). Operational enforcement: regional routing table + mandatory region-check at edge and within the scheduler; KV cache and model shards are pinned to region.
- Fallback behavior: Default behavior is to queue and scale EU capacity. If capacity exceeds configured thresholds we allow configured fallback to equivalent model variant in alternate region (e.g., eu-west -> us-east). For enterprise contracts we can provide an addendum to either:
  a) enable automatic fallback + notification (default), or
  b) include a residency hard-block that rejects requests when EU capacity isn't sufficient (requires commitment to reserved capacity or longer lead times).
- Subprocessors: CSV will include processor name, role (logging, monitoring, storage), country of operation, and whether they receive encrypted payloads.
- DPF evidence: We can provide redacted routing decision logs, edge geolocation headers, and signed timestamps from the scheduler.

I'll attach drafts in a couple hours.

Kimberly Park
Senior Privacy & Compliance Manager
Redwood Inference

Attachments: DPF_Routing_SLA_draft.docx, subprocessor_matrix.csv
From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Claire Evans <claire.evans@meridianbio.com>
Cc: procurement@meridianbio.com, tom.liu@meridianbio.com
Date: 2026-08-17T15:40:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex clarifications — consolidated response

Hi Claire — thanks for the clear list. Please find our consolidated responses below and the referenced artifacts attached. If anything needs more detail we can follow up with a short call.

1) Deterministic routing capabilities
- Commitment: Redwood can include a residency paragraph in the DPA annex that commits to processing customer requests entirely within the EU region(s) specified by Meridian when region-pin is selected. Proposed contract language is included in 'Redwood_DPAAnnex_Table.pdf' (see row 'Residency Commitment - Region Pinning').
- Technical enforcement: requests are accepted at the edge, annotated with customer tenancy and region-pin metadata, and routed through a deterministic routing table in the control plane. Model shards and KV caches for region-pinned workloads are instantiated only in the specified region. We operate an enterprise-only tenancy configuration that prevents cross-region replication for region-pinned tenants.

2) Fallback behavior and opt-out
- Default operational policy: when EU capacity is saturated, the platform will attempt to autoscale EU capacity per the customer's configured autoscale limits. If the request queue exceeds configured thresholds and no EU capacity is available within the configured time window, the system performs one of the following based on the customer's setting:
  a) Allow fallback to an equivalent model variant in a secondary region (with logging and notification), or
  b) Reject the request and return a 409 'region-capacity' response (enterprise-only hard-block).
- Contractual options: we can offer (i) an opt-in fallback with SLA-backed notification and rollback guarantees, or (ii) a hard-block residency guarantee tied to reserved capacity commitments (we recommend reserved capacity for production-critical use-cases).

3) Subprocessor matrix

From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>

Rafael — drafting here. Attachments I'm preparing: DPF_Routing_SLA_draft.docx (text we can include in DPA annex) and subprocessor_matrix.csv (latest). Jordan — can you produce the redacted audit trail by 16:00 ET? Neha — will you confirm whether we can offer a contractual hard-block (no fallback) or only a best-effort notification + opt-out process?

Summary of proposed answers (high-level):
- Deterministic routing: Yes. We offer region-pin routing at the tenant level (customer can require EU-only processing). Operational enforcement: regional routing table + mandatory region-check at edge and within the scheduler; KV cache and model shards are pinned to region.
- Fallback behavior: Default behavior is to queue and scale EU capacity. If capacity exceeds configured thresholds we allow configured fallback to equivalent model variant in alternate region (e.g., eu-west -> us-east). For enterprise contracts we can provide an addendum to either:

- Subprocessors: CSV will include processor name, role (logging, monitoring, storage), country of operation, and whether they receive encrypted payloads.

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Claire Evans <claire.evans@meridianbio.com>
Cc: procurement@meridianbio.com, tom.liu@meridianbio.com
Date: 2026-08-17T15:40:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex clarifications — consolidated response

Hi Claire — thanks for the clear list. Please find our consolidated responses below and the referenced artifacts attached. If anything needs more detail we can follow up with a short call.

1) Deterministic routing capabilities
- Commitment: Redwood can include a residency paragraph in the DPA annex that commits to processing customer requests entirely within the EU region(s) specified by Meridian when region-pin is selected. Proposed contract language is included in 'Redwood_DPAAnnex_Table.pdf' (see row 'Residency Commitment - Region Pinning').
- Technical enforcement: requests are accepted at the edge, annotated with customer tenancy and region-pin metadata, and routed through a deterministic routing table in the control plane. Model shards and KV caches for region-pinned workloads are instantiated only in the specified region. We operate an enterprise-only tenancy configuration that prevents cross-region replication for region-pinned tenants.

2) Fallback behavior and opt-out
- Default operational policy: when EU capacity is saturated, the platform will attempt to autoscale EU capacity per the customer's configured autoscale limits. If the request queue exceeds configured thresholds and no EU capacity is available within the configured time window, the system performs one of the following based on the customer's setting:
  a) Allow fallback to an equivalent model variant in a secondary region (with logging and notification), or
  b) Reject the request and return a 409 'region-capacity' response (enterprise-only hard-block).
- Contractual options: we can offer (i) an opt-in fallback with SLA-backed notification and rollback guarantees, or (ii) a hard-block residency guarantee tied to reserved capacity commitments (we recommend reserved capacity for production-critical use-cases).

3) Subprocessor matrix
- Attached: 'subprocessor_matrix.csv' (columns: subprocessor, service, country, processing_purpose, data_access_level, last_audit). We indicate which subprocessors may process or temporarily cache tokenized content and which only handle telemetry/metrics.

4) DPF evidence and audit artifacts
- We are able to provide the following redacted artifacts under NDA:
  - Routing decision log snippet (timestamped, with region tag and decision reason)
  - Scheduler audit with signed timestamp showing model instance and region assignment
  - Edge geolocation headers showing egress IP and ASN evidence
  - Example of a tenant-level access log showing no cross-region replication for region-pinned tenants
Please note: raw logs will be redacted to remove customer PII and model inputs — example artifacts attached (redacted).

5) DPA annex mapping
- Attached: 'Redwood_DPAAnnex_Table.pdf' with suggested clause text and mapping to typical Meridian controls (SCCs, technical measures, KMS guidance). We propose a Meridian-specific clause that defines 'Region Pinning' and the fallback options above; language includes the option to require reserved capacity.

Timeline: we can provide the attachments and redacted artifacts by end of day 8/17 (attached). If Meridian requires signed artifacts or additional attestation (e.g., SOC2 report excerpts), allow up to 3 business days for legal review and redaction approvals.

Attachments: Redwood_DPAAnnex_Table.pdf, subprocessor_matrix.csv, DPF_Routing_SLA_draft.docx

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Rachael Chen <rachael@acmeanalytics.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 15 Aug 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Demo at KubeCon EU — quick availability check

Hi Rachael,

Thanks again for stopping by the Redwood booth — great to meet you. I wanted to lock in a short 25-minute demo while we're both at KubeCon EU next week. I'm on European time but still fumbling with hotel clocks; my phone kept showing Pacific time after the overnight flight so I may have mixed up the invite windows.

Proposed windows (CEST, Barcelona local):
- Tue 2026-09-21 15:00–15:25 CEST
- Wed 2026-09-22 10:00–10:25 CEST

If neither works, tell me a couple of times that do. I'll send a fresh invite from Jordan once we confirm to avoid timezone flubs. Attaching the latest deck (lightweight) for context.

Attachment: redwood-demo-deck-v2.pdf (PDF, 2.3 MB)

Thanks,
Alex
Alex Martinez
Enterprise Solutions — Redwood Inference
mobile +1 415 555 0199

To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 15 Aug 2026 18:40:00 +0200
Subject: Re: Demo at KubeCon EU — quick availability check

Hi Alex — thanks for the follow-up.

I actually received your calendar invite and it's showing as 07:00 for me (PDT) which made me double-check my own timezone settings. I'm in Barcelona (CEST) for the show and my calendar usually handles timezones, but the hotel front desk reset the clocks in the lobby an hour early this morning because of some maintenance, so a bunch of attendees were confused.

Can you confirm the meeting in local time (CEST) so I can add it to my KubeCon schedule? Either Tue 15:00 CEST or Wed 10:00 CEST works for me — preferably Tue if Alex is ok.

Also CCing the events mailbox in case they want to coordinate space at the booth.

Best,
Rachael
Rachael Chen
Head of Integrations — Acme Analytics

To: Rachael Chen <rachael@acmeanalytics.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 16 Aug 2026 08:12:30 -0700
Subject: Re: Demo at KubeCon EU — quick availability check

Good catch — sorry about the confusion. I just realized when I sent the first invite my calendar defaulted to my home timezone (PDT) instead of the phone's current locale, so the invite slipped into the wrong timezone view. That's entirely my fault — travel brain and hotel clocks didn't help.

I'll ask Jordan to send a fresh invite explicitly in CEST and include an .ics so you can add it to the KubeCon agenda. Laura, can you confirm booth A3 will be quiet enough at Tue 15:00 CEST? If not, Wed 10:00 is our backup.

On CC: Jordan will include the deck link + updated invite.

Apologies again for the mix-up — appreciate your flexibility.

—Alex

Hi Rachael — looping in with corrected calendar details. Thanks for flagging the hotel-clock issue; we've attached an explicit .ics and added both CEST and UTC times in the invite to reduce ambiguity.

New proposed slot (confirmed by Laura for booth A3):
- Tue, 21 Sep 2026 15:00–15:25 CEST (13:00–13:25 UTC) — CONFIRMED

If you prefer the backup, Wed 22 Sep 10:00 CEST is still available.

Attachments:
- kubecon-updated-invite.ics (Calendar invite)
- redwood-demo-deck-v2.pdf (Same deck for quick reference)

Cheers,
Jordan
Senior Solutions Ops — Redwood Inference

Hi Jordan / Alex — confirmed and accepted. Thanks for sending the explicit CEST invite — that did the trick. The front desk did change the clocks early for some reason, so there were a few folks who woke up an hour off. Appreciate the clear UTC fallback in the invite.

source.subject: HelioHub — Executive intro + demo (VP/CIO attendance requested)
messages:
From: Elena Moss <elena.moss@heliohub.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 07 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Quick intro + request for VP/CIO at demo

Hi Ava —

Thanks again for the product overview last week. Our procurement and platform teams are aligned and we'd like to put together a 45-minute executive-level demo next week to show HelioHub's platform and where Redwood would slot into our stack. We generally invite our CTO (Raj Patel) and VP of Platform (Melissa Ng) — they prefer a short executive walkthrough + a 30-minute Q&A focused on reliability, data residency, and cost.

Would Redwood be able to have a senior sponsor (VP-level or above, ideally the person who owns exec briefing) attend? Our first-choice windows next week are:
 - Tue 2026-08-11 between 10:00–12:00 PDT
 - Wed 2026-08-12 14:00–16:00 PDT

I attached a short HelioHub overview and a draft agenda. If those slots don't work we can push to the week after.

Attaching: HelioHub_Overview_2026-08.pdf; also sending calendar stub.

Best,
Elena
Elena Moss
Head of Solutions
HelioHub
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Moss <elena.moss@heliohub.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 07 Aug 2026 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick intro + request for VP/CIO at demo

Elena — thanks for the clear windows and the agenda. Looping in Irene (Director, Field Sponsorship) and Jordan (Solutions Engineer) so we can confirm the right mix for an executive walk-through.

Irene — short ask: HelioHub would like a VP-level sponsor for the demo. Can you cover one of the Tue/Wed windows below? If not, a VP proxy plus a brief pre-brief (15 min) so they can sign off is fine.

Jordan — please review the attached agenda and flag any technical pre-reqs (network/firewall notes, slide add-ons).

Proposed internal options:
 - Irene Choi (VP, Field) — Tue 2026-08-11 10:30–11:15 PDT (can move up to 11:45 if needed)
 - Alternate: Jordan + brief from Irene immediately before (15 min pre-brief at 10:00)

We can confirm once Irene says which she prefers.

Thanks — Ava
Ava Chen
Account Exec, Eastern Region
Redwood Inference
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Elena Moss <elena.moss@heliohub.com>
Cc: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 09:48:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick intro + request for VP/CIO at demo

Thanks — quick technical notes before we lock the slot:

1) Network: if HelioHub intends to show a live integration with their staging cluster we need their IP ranges (or a short-lived SSH tunnel); otherwise screen-share is fine.
2) Data residency: happy to have a single-slide summary showing how Private/VPC deployments look — suggest Irene open on that point.
3) Deck request: can we get the attendee list (titles) and any specific SLA questions up front? Saves time for the Q&A.

Irene — if you're able to cover the VP sponsor role, Tue 08/11 at 10:30 PDT works for me (I'll be on to support the Q&A). If you prefer to pre-brief at 10:00 and have me present as the technical lead at 10:30 I'll run the live demo.

Attachments: heliohub-demo-agenda.ics

—Jordan
Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Moss <elena.moss@heliohub.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 16:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick intro + request for VP/CIO at demo

Elena / team — I can serve as the VP sponsor but travel came up unexpectedly on Tuesday morning and I need to shift the slot by 45 minutes later, if HelioHub can accommodate. Proposed final:

 - Tue 2026-08-11 at 11:15–12:00 PDT (Irene as VP sponsor)

Jordan — happy to do a 10:45–11:00 pre-brief with you (15 min) to run through the residency slide and any asks. Ava — please send the short attendee list and any procurement questions so we can tailor the 10-minute executive summary.

Elena — if 11:15 is tight for HelioHub, we can move to Wed 08/12 14:30–15:15 PDT as a backup.

Irene Choi
VP, Field Sponsorship
Redwood Inference

>  - Alternate: Jordan + brief from Irene immediately before (15 min pre-brief at 10:00)

> We can confirm once Irene says which she prefers.

source.subject: SLA template: uptime guarantee and monthly remedy formula (Synapse)
messages:
From: Dave Allen <dave.allen@synapse.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Synapse Legal <legal@synapse.com>
Date: Fri, 7 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Jordan —

Thanks for sending the initial template. Attached is Synapse's procurement markup and a short list of questions we need clarified before procurement can move to PO. High-level items:

1) Uptime: We're asking for a 99.95% monthly availability commitment for our production cluster (multi-AZ).
2) Remedy: Synapse prefers a pro-rata monthly credit tied to % downtime rather than a fixed cap; they want clear calculation examples.
3) Maintenance: we need a defined maintenance carve-out + a 72-hour advance notice requirement.
4) Regional failover: how will cross-region failovers count toward SLA?

I've attached Synapse_marked_up_SLA.pdf for reference. Can you confirm Redwood's standard stance and whether the remedy calculation below is negotiable?

Synapse proposed remedy (excerpt):
- If monthly availability >= 99.95%: no credit
- 99.90%–99.94%: 5% credit of monthly invoice
- 99.0%–99.89%: 15% credit
- <99.0%: 30% credit

Thanks — happy to hop on a quick call if needed.

Best,
Dave
Procurement Manager | Synapse Systems

Attachment: Synapse_marked_up_SLA.pdf (pdf)
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 7 Aug 2026 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Team — forwarding Synapse's markup and asks. They're pushing for 99.95% availability and a pro-rata credit ladder (see Dave's email). Key negotiation points I want input on:

- Can we accept 99.95% for multi-AZ production in their region (us-west-2) without a major price uplift?
- Remedy structure: their ladder is steeper than our standard. What's the nearest equivalent we can offer that preserves a cap but gives a clearer example table?
- Maintenance and regional failover carve-outs: sync with Product/Infra guidance so we're not over-promising.

I've attached our baseline SLA draft (SLA_Synapse_v1.docx) and created a redlined PDF with my initial comments. Please add quick edits and flag any non-starters. I'm aiming to get a consolidated position back to Dave early next week.

Thanks,
Jordan
Head of Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference

Attachment: SLA_Synapse_v1.docx (docx), SLA_Synapse_v1_redlines.pdf (pdf)
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Jordan / team — my take after reading their ladder:

- 99.95% ask is doable for a committed, dedicated pool (reserved capacity) but not for hosted burst instances. We should tie 99.95% to a Dedicated contract or an uplifted rate. For hosted, 99.9% is our baseline.

- Remedy table: rather than full pro-rata, propose a hybrid: small automatic credit bands plus an annualized cap. Example:
  * >=99.95%: 0%
  * 99.90–99.949%: 3%
  * 99.00–99.899%: 10%
  * <99.00%: 25%
  Cap: monthly credits cannot exceed 50% of monthly invoice (we can propose an alternate cap if they push).

- Maintenance carve-out: we should keep a 48-hour advance notice for scheduled maintenance for service impacting events; for emergency maintenance, define rapid escalation and a postmortem within 7 business days. Synapse's 72-hour ask is fine if they're a Dedicated customer.

- Regional failover: failovers initiated by customer (e.g., their DNS changes) should not count toward Redwood's availability. Automated failover due to our regional outage should be covered.

I'll draft language for the remedy formula and the cap. Flag if you want different numbers before I join the call with Synapse.

-Ben
Sr. Commercial Manager | Redwood Inference
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 11:27:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Ben — +1 on tying 99.95% to Dedicated. A few product/legal notes from my side after scanning the doc: 

1) We need to call out the quantization of maintenance windows (max 4 hours per maintenance event for minor upgrades) and a monthly aggregate (e.g., up to 8 hours/month scheduled maintenance that won't count as downtime).
2) For the credit calculation examples, include an explicit formula: monthly credit = (monthly invoice) * (% downtime bucket credit). Also add a worked example to avoid back-and-forth during procurement.
3) For multi-region failover: clarify that cross-region replication lag that causes degraded performance counts only if it prevents the service from responding to requests (not per-token latency spikes).

I'll add redlines to the doc and propose the hybrid ladder Ben suggested but tighten the cap to 40% for the monthly credit cap (we can negotiate upward if Synapse pushes). Also recommend we offer a one-time operational allowance (first month) if Synapse signs a 12-month Dedicated commitment to help them through cutover.

-Marissa
Director, Product Partnerships | Redwood Inference
From: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
To: Dave Allen <dave.allen@synapse.com>
Cc: Synapse Legal <legal@synapse.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 10 Aug 2026 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft SLA and questions on monthly remedy formula

Dave — thanks for the clear markup and questions. We've reviewed internally and can propose the following consolidated position to keep the procurement process moving: 

1) Availability: Redwood can commit to 99.95% monthly availability for Synapse if the contract is for a Dedicated capacity pool (reserved GPU capacity) in us-west-2. For standard hosted tiers, the availability commitment remains 99.9%.

2) Remedy ladder: we recommend a hybrid ladder with worked examples. Proposed bands:
  - >=99.95%: 0% credit
  - 99.90%–99.949%: 3% of monthly invoice
  - 99.00%–99.899%: 10% of monthly invoice
  - <99.00%: 25% of monthly invoice
  Monthly credit cap: 40% of monthly invoice. (We can revisit the cap if Synapse takes a 12-month Dedicated commitment; in that case we can offer a 50% cap for the first 90 days.)

3) Maintenance carve-out: scheduled maintenance (announced >=48 hours in advance) will be excluded from uptime calculations. We will limit scheduled maintenance to a maximum of 8 hours per month aggregate for production-impacting events and 4 hours max per event for minor upgrades. Emergency maintenance will be documented with a postmortem shared within 7 business days.

4) Regional failover: if Redwood initiates automated failover due to a regional outage, the time during failover will be counted as downtime; customer-initiated failover that results from Synapse changes (DNS, routing) will not count toward Redwood's downtime. Cross-region replication lag will only count if it causes an inability to serve requests.

5) Example calculation: if Synapse's monthly invoice is $100,000 and measured availability for August is 99.85% (downtime bucket 99.00–99.899%), the monthly credit would be 10% -> $10,000, subject to the 40% monthly cap.

Next steps: attached is a redlined SLA draft with the above edits (SLA_Synapse_v1_redlines.pdf) and a versioned doc (SLA_Synapse_v2.docx). Please review and let me know if you'd like a 30-minute call to walk through the bands and cap. If Synapse prefers the original pro-rata ladder, we can discuss trading on a committed volume/term to get closer to their values.

If legal prefers different phrasing for carve-outs or notices, loop them in and we'll iterate quickly.

Thanks — looking forward to finalizing.

Jordan Blake
Head of Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference

Attachments: SLA_Synapse_v1_redlines.pdf (pdf), SLA_Synapse_v2.docx (docx)

source.subject: Perimeter safelist inventory + service mapping for private endpoint onboarding
messages:
From: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@acmehealth.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Cc: secops@acmehealth.com
Date: Wed, 5 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Perimeter safelist inventory + service mapping for private endpoint onboarding

Hi David,

Thanks for the demo last week. Our security team has a narrow onboarding window and needs a compact inventory we can hand to network ops. Attached is our standard safelist questionnaire (acme_safelist_requirements.pdf) — can you provide a mapping that covers: 
- egress destinations (FQDNs and IP ranges) used by the Redwood private control plane and runtime connectors during model serving and telemetry;
- TCP/UDP ports and protocol expectations (including ephemeral ranges if applicable);
- source IPs or NAT pools we should allow from our perimeter to reach the private endpoint(s);
- TLS expectations (SNI needed? mTLS requirements?) and how our middleboxes should inspect traffic without breaking signed requests;
- brief description of fallback behavior: if dedicated IPs are unavailable, will traffic use a regional pool or fail closed?

We prefer a single spreadsheet with columns for service, fqdn, ip, port, direction, justification. If you can attach the file and a short narrative, we can route to our firewall change board by EOD Friday (2026-08-06).

Thanks,
Alex Morgan
Head of Security Integrations
Acme Health

--
Attachment: acme_safelist_requirements.pdf (form)

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 5 Aug 2026 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Perimeter safelist inventory + service mapping for private endpoint onboarding

Hi Alex — appreciate the clear ask. Attached is the initial inventory (fw_port_map.xlsx) that maps our services to FQDN/IPs/ports and identifies the control plane vs runtime traffic. Key notes up front: 

- Control plane (control.redwood.cloud and *.control.redwood.cloud): outbound TCP 443 from the private runtime to our control plane for heartbeats, telemetry, and orchestration. We recommend allowing SNI for TLS inspection but not TLS termination; we do not require mTLS for the control plane by default (we support it for private deployments on request).

- Runtime model traffic: depending on your deployment mode (VPC peered private endpoint vs connector NAT), runtime requests may egress to a small set of regional IPs. The spreadsheet lists both the current static IP blocks and an ephemeral fallback pool we use for bursts. For hard allowlist customers we can reserve static NATs as part of the Dedicated plan — please let us know if you need guaranteed source IPs.

- Ports: primary is TCP 443 (HTTPS). For health checks we use outbound TCP 80 in some legacy probes (listed as optional in the sheet). No UDP required for inference or control-plane operations.

- FQDN vs IP: we publish signed FQDNs and advise allowing FQDN-based safelisting for better long-term stability; where customers mandate IP-only allowlists we provide an IP snapshot and a weekly delta feed.

- Fallback behavior: by default, if a pinned IP is unreachable, traffic will fallback to our regional pool and we will surface incident alerts. If you require fail-closed behavior, please indicate that in the sheet and we will disable automatic fallback for your account and provide reserved NATs.

Next steps I propose: Jordan — can you confirm the final source NAT pools and an attestation doc for the static IPs? Marissa — can you prepare a one-page attestation (signed) describing our operational cadence for IP rotation and delta feed publishing? Alex — I attached fw_port_map.xlsx with columns: Service, Role, FQDN, Current IPs, Fallback IP Pool, Ports, Protocol, Justification, Notes.

I can join your firewall change board call Thursday morning if that helps speed approval.

Best,
David Kim
Head of Product Security, Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: fw_port_map.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 6 Aug 2026 08:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Internal: action items for Acme Health safelist

Jordan / Marissa — forwarding Acme's original and my reply. Short checklist so we can close this by EOD Thu: 

1) Jordan: verify the IP blocks in fw_port_map.xlsx against our NAT registry and mark which pools are "reserved-static" vs "regional-ephemeral". Put the confirmed list in column 'Confirmed IPs' and sign the audit line.

2) Marissa: produce the attestation PDF describing rotation cadence, where we publish the delta feed (S3 signed list + weekly digest), and the contact for incident change notifications. We need that in the format Acme's ops team expects (one page + signature block).

3) I'll join the Acme firewall board at 10:00 PT if your outputs are ready by 09:45 PT.

A few clarifications to include in the attestation template: 
- We do not require customers to terminate TLS at the perimeter; SNI-based allowlisting is the recommended inspection pattern.
- We support mTLS for private control planes if Acme purchases the Dedicated package — note that enabling mTLS will require certificate exchange and an onboarding window.
- For customers wanting fail-closed (no fallback), we will provide a static NAT lease and a cutover checklist; that needs sign-off from sales/legal for contract amendment (deal id: ACME-2026-PRIV-42).

Jordan, please reply inline on the sheet when you confirm. Marissa, I'll drop a starter attestation in Google Drive for you to edit: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3cAcmeSafelist

Thanks — David

From: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@acmehealth.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Cc: secops@acmehealth.com
Date: Thu, 6 Aug 2026 16:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Perimeter safelist inventory + service mapping for private endpoint onboarding

David — thank you, this is exactly what we needed. We've reviewed fw_port_map.xlsx and the notes. A few acceptances and one ask: 

- Acceptance: control.redwood.cloud (443) plus the listed runtime FQDNs/ips are approved for our firewall board.
- Acceptance: TLS SNI-based inspection allowed; we will not terminate TLS for Redwood connections.
- Ask: for the static NAT pool you marked as reserved-static, please provide the signed attestation PDF so our change board can stamp the change ticket.

I've attached the change ticket reference to this thread and pushed your sheet into our Jira ticket (see HubSpot: 4632). If you can drop the attestation before 09:30 PT tomorrow, we'll schedule the configuration window for Friday 08/07.

Best regards,
Alex Morgan
Head of Security Integrations
Acme Health

--
Attachment: Jira-CHG-9421 (change ticket link)

Subject: Perimeter safelist inventory + service mapping for private endpoint onboarding

Hi David,

Thanks for the demo last week. Our security team has a narrow onboarding window and needs a compact inventory we can hand to network ops. Attached is our standard safelist questionnaire (acme_safelist_requirements.pdf) — can you provide a mapping that covers: 
- egress destinations (FQDNs and IP ranges) used by the Redwood private control plane and runtime connectors during model serving and telemetry;
- TCP/UDP ports and protocol expectations (including ephemeral ranges if applicable);
- source IPs or NAT pools we should allow from our perimeter to reach the private endpoint(s);
- TLS expectations (SNI needed? mTLS requirements?) and how our middleboxes should inspect traffic without breaking signed requests;
- brief description of fallback behavior: if dedicated IPs are unavailable, will traffic use a regional pool or fail closed?

We prefer a single spreadsheet with columns for service, fqdn, ip, port, direction, justification. If you can attach the file and a short narrative, we can route to our firewall change board by EOD Friday (2026-08-06).

Thanks,
Alex Morgan
Head of Security Integrations
Acme Health

--
Attachment: acme_safelist_requirements.pdf (form)

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 5 Aug 2026 11:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Perimeter safelist inventory + service mapping for private endpoint onboarding

Hi Alex — appreciate the clear ask. Attached is the initial inventory (fw_port_map.xlsx) that maps our services to FQDN/IPs/ports and identifies the control plane vs runtime traffic. Key notes up front: 

- Control plane (control.redwood.cloud and *.control.redwood.cloud): outbound TCP 443 from the private runtime to our control plane for heartbeats, telemetry, and orchestration. We recommend allowing SNI for TLS inspection but not TLS termination; we do not require mTLS for the control plane by default (we support it for private deployments on request).

- Runtime model traffic: depending on your deployment mode (VPC peered private endpoint vs connector NAT), runtime requests may egress to a small set of regional IPs. The spreadsheet lists both the current static IP blocks and an ephemeral fallback pool we use for bursts. For hard allowlist customers we can reserve static NATs as part of the Dedicated plan — please let us know if you need guaranteed source IPs.

- Ports: primary is TCP 443 (HTTPS). For health checks we use outbound TCP 80 in some legacy probes (listed as optional in the sheet). No UDP required for inference or control-plane operations.

- FQDN vs IP: we publish signed FQDNs and advise allowing FQDN-based safelisting for better long-term stability; where customers mandate IP-only allowlists we provide an IP snapshot and a weekly delta feed.

- Fallback behavior: by default, if a pinned IP is unreachable, traffic will fallback to our regional pool and we will surface incident alerts. If you require fail-closed behavior, please indicate that in the sheet and we will disable automatic fallback for your account and provide reserved NATs.

Next steps I propose: Jordan — can you confirm the final source NAT pools and an attestation doc for the static IPs? Marissa — can you prepare a one-page attestation (signed) describing our operational cadence for IP rotation and delta feed publishing? Alex — I attached fw_port_map.xlsx with columns: Service, Role, FQDN, Current IPs, Fallback IP Pool, Ports, Protocol, Justification, Notes.

I can join your firewall change board call Thursday morning if that helps speed approval.

Best,
David Kim
Head of Product Security, Redwood Inference

--
Attachment: fw_port_map.xlsx (spreadsheet)

From: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 6 Aug 2026 08:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Internal: action items for Acme Health safelist

Jordan / Marissa — forwarding Acme's original and my reply. Short checklist so we can close this by EOD Thu: 

1) Jordan: verify the IP blocks in fw_port_map.xlsx against our NAT registry and mark which pools are "reserved-static" vs "regional-ephemeral". Put the confirmed list in column 'Confirmed IPs' and sign the audit line.

2) Marissa: produce the attestation PDF describing rotation cadence, where we publish the delta feed (S3 signed list + weekly digest), and the contact for incident change notifications. We need that in the format Acme's ops team expects (one page + signature block).

3) I'll join the Acme firewall board at 10:00 PT if your outputs are ready by 09:45 PT.

A few clarifications to include in the attestation template: 
- We do not require customers to terminate TLS at the perimeter; SNI-based allowlisting is the recommended inspection pattern.
- We support mTLS for private control planes if Acme purchases the Dedicated package — note that enabling mTLS will require certificate exchange and an onboarding window.
- For customers wanting fail-closed (no fallback), we will provide a static NAT lease and a cutover checklist; that needs sign-off from sales/legal for contract amendment (deal id: ACME-2026-PRIV-42).

Jordan, please reply inline on the sheet when you confirm. Marissa, I'll drop a starter attestation in Google Drive for you to edit: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3cAcmeSafelist

Thanks — David

From: Alex Morgan <alex.morgan@acmehealth.com>
To: David Kim <david.kim@redwood.com>
Cc: secops@acmehealth.com
Date: Thu, 6 Aug 2026 16:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Perimeter safelist inventory + service mapping for private endpoint onboarding

David — thank you, this is exactly what we needed. We've reviewed fw_port_map.xlsx and the notes. A few acceptances and one ask: 

- Acceptance: control.redwood.cloud (443) plus the listed runtime FQDNs/ips are approved for our firewall board.
- Acceptance: TLS SNI-based inspection allowed; we will not terminate TLS for Redwood connections.
- Ask: for the static NAT pool you marked as reserved-static, please provide the signed attestation PDF so our change board can stamp the change ticket.

I've attached the change ticket reference to this thread and pushed your sheet into our Jira ticket (see HubSpot: 4632). If you can drop the attestation before 09:30 PT tomorrow, we'll schedule the configuration window for Friday 08/07.

Best regards,
Alex Morgan
Head of Security Integrations
Acme Health

--
Attachment: Jira-CHG-9421 (change ticket link)

source.subject: Request: BCP/DR evidence for RTO/RPO — BlueBay security review
messages:
From: Tom Leiter <tom.leiter@bluebay.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: BCP/DR evidence request — RTO/RPO and runbook artifacts

Hi Camila,

Thanks for taking this on. As part of BlueBay's vendor security review, we need evidence around Redwood's business continuity and disaster recovery capabilities specific to the hosted inference product that our team will consume. Primary asks:

- RTO & RPO targets by deployment type (hosted, dedicated, private) and any measured results from the last 12 months.
- A current DR runbook index (table of contents) and one example drill log.
- Evidence of warm-cache/k-v cache strategies for reducing cold-start RTO (screenshots or sample logs are fine).
- Recent sample audit logs that show failover & restore activity (anonymized).

Can you provide a single bundle or point me to where these artifacts live? Ideally by end of this week — our internal reviewers are on a tight schedule.

Tom Leiter
Security Assurance Manager
BlueBay Finance

Attachments: none
From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.com>
To: Tom Leiter <tom.leiter@bluebay.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 3 Aug 2026 11:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: BCP/DR evidence request — RTO/RPO and runbook artifacts

Hi Tom —

Thanks for the clear checklist. Quick summary up front: we can share a consolidated evidence bundle containing the RTO/RPO summary, DR runbook index, one drill log, warm-cache screenshots, and a small sample of audit logs (anonymized). I’ve attached the primary artifacts to this reply: rto_rpo_summary_v1.pdf and runbook_index_v2.xlsx.

Notes:
- RTO/RPO: Our published targets are in the summary PDF; we include measured percentiles from the last 12 months for hosted and dedicated. For Private (on-prem) we provide guidance and customer-run metrics — we note typical operational variance there.
- Runbook: runbook_index_v2.xlsx is a TOC + contact matrix and a link to the full runbook folder (see Drive link below).
- Warm cache: we can provide KV cache warmup screenshots and short CloudTrail snippets — I can add those in a follow-up if you want them embedded or as separate files.

If you need redacted drill logs that include timestamps and operator actions (we can remove ticket IDs), let me know the required fields and I’ll redact before sending.

I’ve CC’d Jordan (Ops) for technical clarifications; Jordan, can you attach the KV/cache screenshots and a small anonymized cloudtrail sample?

Thanks,

--
Camila Reyes
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
camila.reyes@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0142

Attachments: rto_rpo_summary_v1.pdf, runbook_index_v2.xlsx
From: Tom Leiter <tom.leiter@bluebay.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.com>
Cc: BlueBay Security Team <security@bluebay.com>
Date: Wed, 4 Aug 2026 08:27:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: BCP/DR evidence request — RTO/RPO and runbook artifacts

On Wed, Aug 4, 2026 at 8:08 AM Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Tom —
> 
> Thanks for the clear checklist. Quick summary up front: we can share a consolidated evidence bundle...

Thanks Camila — that looks like a good start. A couple of follow-ups from the review team:

1) For hosted inference, can you provide the 95th and 99th percentile observed RTO during the last three region failover tests (if possible, timestamped)? We understand you may need to redact request identifiers.
2) For the Private (on-prem) deployment option, the reviewer wants to confirm whether KV cache priming is customer-driven or if Redwood offers an automated warm-start during failover. A short sentence on the operational responsibility split would help.
3) Please include one drill log that shows a failover initiated by Redwood (not just customer) if available — we can accept redacted operator handles.

If you can get the additional screenshots and the anonymized cloudtrail snippet, we can close this item.

Tom

Attachments: none
From: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.com>
To: Tom Leiter <tom.leiter@bluebay.com>
Cc: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 5 Aug 2026 15:47:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: BCP/DR evidence request — RTO/RPO and runbook artifacts

Tom —

Following up with the technical details you asked for. See the attached artifacts and the Drive folder linked below (we have a read-only collection for security reviews). Highlights:

- Hosted (regional failover tests): observed RTO (time from failover start to end-to-end inference validation): 95th pct = 5m 20s, 99th pct = 8m 45s (across three scheduled drills in us-west-2/us-east-1/eu-west-1; timestamps are in the attached runbook drill-log.csv with request IDs redacted).
- Dedicated: measured 95th pct RTO = 4m 10s, 99th pct = 6m 5s (note: dedicated pools have reserved capacity and automated priming).
- Private/on-prem: Redwood provides an optional warm-start agent that can be deployed by customers; for managed Private installs (contracted), Redwood can operate the warm-starts as part of an ops addendum. Operational responsibility is listed in the runbook_index_v2.xlsx under section 2.4.
- Warm-cache evidence: attached kv_warm_cache_screenshots.png (cache hit-rate graphs during a planned failover) and cloudtrail_sample.log (anonymized).

Drive (read-only): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aBcD_example_runbook
DocuSign (statement): https://app.docusign.com/BB-4352/evidence-statement

If you want a redacted CSV of the drill log in a specific schema, tell us the fields and we’ll produce it; otherwise the attached CSV contains timestamps, stage markers, and operator notes.

Regards,

Diego Fernandez
Director, Reliability Engineering | Redwood Inference
diego.fernandez@redwood.com

Attachments: kv_warm_cache_screenshots.png, cloudtrail_sample.log, runbook_drill-log.csv
